output: Mr. President, Mr. President, Madam Vice-President, the Commission gives the impression of acting like a distracted student rushing to catch up with lost time before handing in their exam paper.
Certainly, the social package exists, but it is incomplete and can only be regarded as a step toward the Commission's consideration of social issues.

The President of the Commission failed to heed the warning issued by citizens in France, the Netherlands, and Ireland, which would unfortunately have been identical elsewhere had there been a popular vote.
These elections are reminders to get back on track.
If Europeans want more Europe, it is to build a social project, not merely to adopt yet more technical directives.
Regulation concerning the internal market is indeed necessary, but it is by no means the essential element.
The 2009 program, like its predecessors, will miss the point.
Citizens do not expect the Commission to merely complete, let alone simplify, legislation.
They expect the Commission to live up to the task assigned to it by the treaties—the locomotive, the think tank of the Union.
It is not enough for its president to be wise and silent, standing at attention behind the President of the Council.
We expect the Commission to act as a key player in reflecting on the nature of the societal project we need.
But what is the societal project behind this program?
The 2009 programme contains no major errors, but it is not the programme our fellow citizens need.
Mr. President, it will be up to the new college to assume its responsibilities.
I only hope he will be less deaf to the appeals of peoples than the current one.
Mr. President, first of all, a question for the Council.

the guidelines of the high-level conference on Georgia, which had been proposed by the French Presidency, and how the current EU Presidency intends to uphold Georgia's territorial integrity, despite a country recognizing two separatist regions, Abkhazia and South Ossetia.
But it's a pity that Mr. Barroso is not here, because in his speech he was unconvincing, perhaps because he himself is not convinced by the proposals he has made to us.
I believe the financial and economic situation is far more serious than he has described it to us.
Even today, I believe those who follow the internet clearly see that stock markets continue to collapse despite intervention.

It is truly, for me, the collapse of the current system—a system entirely obsolete, the system that led to bankruptcy, that caused the earth to be completely exploited and suffocated by greenhouse gas emissions, that triggered underdevelopment in the Global South, and that has caused underdevelopment in our cities as well.
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, the current financial crisis clearly shows that when the issue is global, a global response is required.
And this is true, especially in the field of agriculture or in the food sector.
This is why, in particular, the World Trade Organization was established, which we agree upon, and which seeks to implement globally applicable rules.

It happens that this does not quite work as we would wish, because we are faced with the following problem: we must reconcile two necessities—the free trade that everyone agrees is essential, and the protections that are equally necessary.
and the protection of national economies or agriculture does not mean protectionism.
The path we have followed over the past 60 years, since GATT, has been an unbalanced one, aimed at reducing customs tariffs with the objective of eliminating them entirely.
It so happens that, technically, the reduction of customs duties
It's quite difficult, as evidenced by the fact that there is an entire series of mathematical formulas—the South Korean formula, the European formula, or the most well-known one, the Swiss formula.
It doesn't really work because a product is not unitary.
It comprises numerous elements with rules originating from very different sources, and technically reducing customs duties is not as straightforward as one might think.
We are therefore in a technical deadlock, and politically, through negotiations, we attempt to emerge from this impasse.
Now, it so happens that, technologically, a new customs technology has been developed: the technology of customs duties deductible by the exporter from the economy of the importing state.
In practice, this deductible customs duty is transformed into a customs credit equal to the amount of the customs duty paid by the exporter in the importing country.
This three-character customs credit is refundable, negotiable, and eligible for discounting.
It is refundable at the moment the exporter purchases from the importer, at which point they may deduct the duty they have paid.
It is negotiable because if the exporting company that has paid 100 in customs duties has nothing to purchase from the importer, it can negotiate the customs credit on the stock market or in a bank.
It is beneficial if we wish to assist developing country importers in offering a customs duty amount higher than the amount of customs duty.
Well, with this technique, we create an international trade currency whose money supply equals the amount of existing customs duties.
For example, the European Union would provide 13 billion euros of international currency in the commercial sector.

Mr. President, the Russo-Georgian conflict and the financial crisis provided the European Union with the opportunity to establish itself on the world stage as a political actor in its own right and of the highest order.
Thanks to your determination, Mr. President of the Council, Europe has succeeded in uniting and finding coordinated, effective, and swift responses.
In the face of crises, Europe proves that it exists and demonstrates its added value.
In the matter of immigration, for example, individual self-interest was no longer acceptable.
The adoption by the European Council of the migration and asylum pact, initiated by the French presidency, represents a significant advancement.
On the fight against climate change, Europe must set concrete goals and show the way to its global partners.

You have already expressed your views on this matter, Mr. President of the Council, but we wish this agreement to be as balanced as possible, as it must take into account the economic situation of our countries, which are currently very unstable.
but also pose the question of the type of development we will choose for the future.
To meet the challenges ahead, the European Union needs the Treaty of Lisbon more than ever.
We understand the concerns of our Irish friends, but the status quo is not an option.
Ireland must offer us a solution at the December European Council; many of us are calling for this.
In recent weeks, we have proudly observed that a strong, politically united Europe, determined in the face of adversity and resolute with its partners, is now being heard on the international stage.
We wish for this new European mindset to become the norm and to endure.
The Treaty of Lisbon is the best solution we have at our disposal to achieve this.
Madam President, Mr. Minister, I was in Tbilisi when President Sarkozy came to negotiate the peace plan, and of course I pay tribute to him for acting swiftly and managing to halt this war.
But this war is, in a way, our failure. For fourteen years, we were hesitant, silently watching the escalation of provocations in the separatist regions.

This war has indeed awakened Europe, forcing it to confront its responsibilities; however, the fire still smolders in the Caucasus, and we must do everything possible to finally resolve the frozen conflicts for the security of all Europe.
I am also aware, Mr. Minister, of the divisions among European countries regarding Georgia's accession to NATO, and personally, I am opposed to it.
I will make you a proposal; I will ask the European Union to propose the status of neutrality for these Caucasus countries.
Only the status of neutrality will be able to ease tensions with Russia and definitively prevent this subregion from further conflicts.
This status will ensure the security of these young democracies and contribute to our own security.
Mr. President, Mr. Minister, over the past three days, the European Development Days have taken place, during which the severe food crisis affecting the poorest countries was widely discussed.
All interventions emphasized the absolute necessity for developing countries to develop their own agriculture by optimally utilizing their land and natural resources.

In passing, the lyrical outbursts of those who called yesterday and still call today for reducing state intervention and public spending, for liberalizing the entire Asian continent, for privatization, and for relegating agriculture.
This near-prehistoric practice, this sudden reversal therefore favoring massive public investments in agricultural production and food sovereignty, all of it was simultaneously absurd and scandalous.
The future of agriculture in developing countries is directly linked to the debate we are having today.

Unfortunately, I do not sense that this health check of the CAP, initiated before the peak of the food riots and the disastrous market upheavals, takes into account the situation of southern farming communities or draws all the necessary conclusions from the severe crises we are currently experiencing—ecological, social, economic, and financial.

I therefore deeply regret that the strong relationship between farmers from the North and the South is so little addressed, both in the Commission's proposals and in the Parliament's report, and that the challenge to the promised one billion euros underscores just how far the journey still is.
Mr. President, the report we are about to vote on tomorrow marks the culmination of a year of work and negotiations devoted to the future of the common agricultural policy and of our farmers.
Indeed, it was last 20 November that Mrs. Fischer Boel came to present her proposals on the health check-up report.
Following Lutz Goebbels' interim report, here is Capula Santo's report on the Commission's proposal.
We must congratulate our two colleagues on their excellent work.
The European Parliament's Agriculture Committee has managed to remain firm in the face of the Commission's initial proposals, which we considered far too liberal.
Madam President, I will remain vigilant regarding the milk sector, and in particular, the measures preceding the abolition of quotas.

I am particularly thinking of mountain dairy producers, who must not become the neglected beneficiaries of this reform; we must ensure they are not left behind, as they will require greater support than others.
I don't want to take unnecessary risks, but how can one imagine the French dairy industry concentrated within a single region?
I therefore welcome with goodwill the proposal to establish a milk fund, as well as the one calling for assessing, as of 2010, the increase in milk quotas according to market developments.

Mr. President, the calculation of the progressive modulation carried out by the Agriculture Committee appears correct to me and would enable us to strengthen the development of territories, rural areas, and to sustainably preserve biodiversity.
I also commend the clarification of eco-conditionality, which until now has been a genuine headache for farmers to implement.

I also wish for us to maintain the amount of the tobacco fund, which enables us to preserve our small farms across Europe, as well as a significant workforce in a region where nothing else is viable, and which helps us avoid relying on imported products.
Finally, Mr. Minister, we greatly appreciated your commitment throughout these negotiations.
You have shown great patience and great determination, and you have not spared your time without error.
It was a genuine pleasure to work alongside you.
You have been highly effective with us.
I know that we can rely on
Mr. President, thank you first of all for the work completed and the conclusions and recommendations proposed in the health check-up of the CAP, as presented in Mr. Cabulos Santos's report.
Agriculture is indeed an economic sector that cannot respond to mere market signals.
Its activity inevitably has impacts, of course, on food self-sufficiency, but also, as we too often see, on territories and on employment.
The removal of market instruments would be a serious blow to our agriculture.

Decoupling, for example, poses a genuine challenge for certain livestock regions, particularly those specializing in dairy cattle and sheep, and I am pleased to have heard Mrs. Fischer Boel address this issue earlier.
Moreover, it is clear that strictly maintaining historical references would greatly hinder any possibility of reform.
Nevertheless, I still believe that the issue of the second pillar of the CAP is essential, because rural areas need to rely on agricultural activity to develop employment in rural regions.
One must therefore consider this second issue without in any way undermining the first.
Given that Minister Jouillet is about to leave us, I believe this Parliament should thank him.
He was one of the most diligent ministers we have ever known.
I wish him good luck.

Madam President, I voted in favor of the Perisch report on the integration of Océane 20 into the unified Océane, though I must say with deep regret, as I believe this unified Océane does not represent a simplification and does not enhance transparency.
it will make life more complicated for vineyards across the entire wine industry.
Last night, the commissioner tried to reassure us.
I hope the Commission will keep its promise and that, above all, the profession will continue to be adequately represented on the advisory committee, as has been the case since the first WTO.
- He is a real gentleman.
Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, our group enthusiastically supports the proposal for the Small Business Act that the Commission presented to us last June.

Indeed, since the 2000s, initiatives have been undertaken targeting SMEs, and I particularly think of the excellent work by our colleague Dominique Vlastoo on the SME Charter. These initiatives were appreciated but deemed insufficient, as they often remained little more than pious wishes.
Today, we must go further.
The severe economic crisis our countries are experiencing, whose consequences are particularly worrying for SMEs, invites us to do so, if further encouragement were needed.
In this regard, the Small Business Act represents a new approach, decidedly proactive.
In a few weeks, we will examine Mrs. Herzog's report on the Commission's communication, but today, through the submission of this oral question, we wished to address the Council.
Mr. Minister, we have noted with interest the conclusions adopted yesterday by the Competitiveness Council, and we would like to know whether the European Council of 11 and 12 December next will

will firmly uphold its conclusions and provide strong impetus to ensure that the overarching guiding principle of "think small first" is rapidly incorporated into the regulatory framework of the Member States.
We wish for the Council to commit to the principle of the attached action plan, accompanied by concrete measures.
In this regard, and within the limited time available to me, I will emphasize three directions in which we need binding impulses.

Impact assessments, which should be systematic and followed by regular monitoring, with parliamentary oversight by the European Parliament regarding the implementation of the Small Business Act in the Member States.
access to public procurement
The code of conduct has no binding force. We must together be more imaginative, more innovative, and propose concrete, appropriate measures that genuinely open up these markets to SMEs.
Finally, access to financing, more indispensable than ever in the current context.
Your proposals are good, Mr. Minister; they must also include support for supply.
Urgency.
Please ensure us of the Council's intention.

Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Commissioner, we all know that small and medium-sized enterprises are the backbone of the European Union, as they employ over 70% of the Union's workforce and account for 99% of European businesses.

However, as already mentioned, these millions of small and medium-sized enterprises are now troubled and threatened by the financial crisis, as it erodes household purchasing power and confidence, and affects order books.

It is therefore the cash flow of SMEs that is at risk, and consequently, efforts must be made to support their operations by ensuring that banks guarantee effective access to the financing necessary for the continuity of their activities.

This is precisely the central issue addressed in the conclusions of the Competitiveness Council of 1 December, which the Minister mentioned, as well as in the recovery plan approved by the European ministers of finance.
Making the needs of SMEs a permanent political priority for the Union is essential, and I would like to commend the action plan in support of the Small Business Act for Europe.
It indeed reflects the intention to ensure the effective implementation of the guiding principle of giving priority to small ones first, that is, to European SMEs.
However, as the Commissioner has said, and as we all maintain, the Council must genuinely engage with the other institutions.
European Union: for this principle to become effective now, we must move beyond declaration and proceed to implementation across all European policies and programmes.
However, we can say that this action plan represents a genuine response to the urgency of the situation.
And, Mr. Minister, I would like to commend the determination of the French Presidency of the Union, which has enabled intentions to be swiftly translated into action.

Mr. President, Madam President-in-Office, Mr. Commissioner, I fully support the rapporteur in defending the position that was ours during the first reading, a position rejected by the governments of the Member States.
Mr. President, it was urgent to bring our legislation on parental leave into line with the case law of the Court of Justice; this has now been accomplished.
The CERCAS report provides balanced and employee-protective responses.
All time spent in care is counted as working time.
The compensatory rest takes place immediately after the service period.
It is a matter of common sense to ensure decent working conditions, particularly for medical professions.
But the reform of the Working Time Directive also provides us with the opportunity to advance our European social law by abolishing the individual opt-out.
The CERCAS report seizes this opportunity and proposes a gradual elimination of any possibility of derogation from the legal maximum working time.
One must face reality squarely.
It is a joke to claim that the employee is in a position of equality with their employer and can refuse what is offered to them.
Dear colleagues, we must clearly show the governments of the member states that the text they wish to impose on us is unacceptable.

In the chorus of praise that will undoubtedly soon soothe the ears of the French government, I believe it is essential first and foremost to focus on the employees who will be compelled to work ever more, without truly having a choice, as well as on all those who will be asked to work on Sundays in France.
Indeed, it is clearly to open this possibility that the French government changed its position on individual opt-out at the Council.

Mr. President, dear colleagues, let us listen to the workers and respond to their call, if we do not wish the negative votes in the French, Dutch, and Irish referendums to be followed by many others, undermining an European Union that leaves them with the feeling that their daily concerns are not being addressed.
Mr. President, this was a very successful French presidency.
There were very few dissenting voices in failing to agree.
The twenty-seven set aside their differences to reach a series of decisions that were exemplary.
On the climate-energy package, Europe is taking its first step, and in this regard, that first step is immense.
She now has the means to assume her environmental responsibility without compromising her economy.
She can now take the lead in this matter, guiding the other continents toward sustainable development.
Moreover, Ireland proposes a scenario for overcoming its isolation.
Our Irish friends will once again vote on the Lisbon Treaty with all the cards on the table, since practically all the other members will have already made their final decision.
Current crises demonstrate that the Union more than ever needs the best institutional tools to be effective.
The European Council adopted a large-scale, coordinated economic recovery plan, based on long-term productive investment and targeted support for the most severely affected industrial sectors.
President Sarkozy raised the issue of reducing certain VAT rates, and I fully share his viewpoint.
Let us hope that the ministers of economics and finance will reach an agreement in March, an outcome that seemed impossible to the heads of state and government last weekend.
The challenges facing Europeans provide the Union with the opportunity to reveal itself to them as more united, more responsive, and more committed to protecting them.
We are grateful to the French Presidency for having injected this new momentum.
We wish, like Nicolas Sarkozy, never again to attend the kind of late-night meetings of yesterday that dragged on until four in the morning just to negotiate a few trifles.
Over the past six months, Europe has indeed undergone a change in mindset, scope, and credibility.
We must thank the French Presidency for this; we are fully aware that it is by no means entirely foreign.
The situation in Gaza is known to everyone.
What we are discussing here in Parliament is natural.

But we are demanding that the Security Council take a stance, that the institutions take a stance, that the European Union take a stance, and we, in Parliament, are discussing and do not wish to take a position.

I believe that, despite everything, given the urgency of the situation in Gaza, it is of utmost importance that this Parliament speaks out and clearly states exactly what it wants in order to put an end to the massacres currently taking place in the Middle East.
I find unacceptable that this Parliament lacks the courage and clarity to adopt a resolution following our debate.
That is why our group calls for the decision of the Presidents' Conference to be revised and for us to conclude the debate that we have, and must have.
We must address the situation in Gaza through a resolution that clearly and firmly demonstrates this Parliament's majority stance demanding an end to the massacres in Gaza.

We want a resolution; we must be aware that we bear a political responsibility regarding what is happening, and this political responsibility cannot merely be a discussion—it must be a resolution that clearly states what we want and what we condemn.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, more than ever, the proposed directive which the Parliament is about to vote on evokes in me a very mixed feeling.
It is true that the rapporteur, Mr. Klinz, has carried out extensive work over many years and has negotiated skillfully, particularly during the trilogue meetings.

It is also true that the directive represents a number of significant advancements, moving toward a better functioning of the internal market and improved collective management of financial assets, and has been welcomed as such by the European investment fund industry.

It remains true that debates and discussions within the Council and the Parliament were never entirely free, since the outcome was known in advance. For certain actors, it was imperative—no matter the cost—to introduce the European passport for management companies.
On principle, there is nothing to object to—the passport holds up quite well. However, one must ensure that the introduction of the passport for management companies
It is accompanied by necessary safeguards, particularly regarding fund supervision, as it leads to a separation of functions across borders.
Indeed, I regret to observe that the system implemented is not only complex, but also subject to divergent interpretations.
It may quickly encounter practical difficulties that will harm the European fund industry's ability to export, particularly exports to third countries.
I fully acknowledge that my position is in the minority, but I do not feel uncomfortable as a result.
Under normal circumstances, I would have been tempted to say that only practice will allow us to draw definitive conclusions on this matter and thus determine
Who is right between those who trust the market participants' ability to adapt and those who adopt a more cautious approach?
It is not impossible that success lies ahead, but on the other hand, nothing guarantees it either.
Moreover, the very recent events—particularly the Madoff scandal and its implications for the collective asset management industry—do little to reassure us.
Skepticism gives way to my unease.
The investment fund industry will not be spared by the financial crisis; we are already observing this firsthand.
Fundamental reassessments may be necessary in this context of crisis.

That, in this context of profound crisis, we make choices that dilute the responsibilities of the actors or assume perfect cooperation among regulatory authorities can truly be regarded as incongruous.
I tell you this as I truly believe it: this directive reflects the mindset of a bygone era, prior to the crisis.
It is marked by a certain carelessness.
Indeed, the financial system is going to experience a lasting and fundamental crisis of confidence, and entire sections of its architecture must be rethought.

Let us not forget, the primary function of UCITS is to protect the investor through risk diversification and the establishment of strict rules. By voting for this text in its current form, without truly knowing where we are headed, we are acting as though nothing has happened.
This autism portends nothing good; that is why I shall refrain, even though I have signed the compromise amendments negotiated with the Council.
And given the circumstances and the improvements made, it seems to me that we have scarcely addressed the fundamental issues I have just outlined.

Mr. President, although the regulation on animal reporting has been in effect since January 2007, it appears that Member States are not systematically complying by submitting the annual reports requested of them.
Moreover, a comprehensive analysis remains unreliable, as many details are missing regarding the resources deployed to carry out inspections.
Mr. President, member of animal protection associations and member of the parliamentary group for animal welfare, this issue particularly concerns me.
For decades, associations have been desperately fighting to establish guidelines in this area, where it has become evident that Member States display a certain laxity.
I would even go so far as to speak of a certain unwillingness, because after all, we know that implementing controls and inspections is by no means insurmountable.
We know the slaughterhouses, we know the breeding sites, we know the livestock transit routes—so where is the problem?

I believe it is important, in our era when public opinion is increasingly, and rightly, sensitive to animal welfare—whether in terms of breeding, slaughter, or transport conditions—that states respect these sentiments.

Since I have the floor, I would like to add, with regard to transportation, that it would be essential to examine and take into account, independently of transportation times, the local climatic conditions.
Indeed, a several-hour journey undertaken in the Netherlands during spring bears no comparison to the same duration of travel during peak summer in a country such as Greece.
Shouldn't nighttime transportation be imposed in these final cases?
I thank you for considering this proposal in the future.

Madam President, I am pleased that it is a British colleague, our fellow Member Parrish, who has tabled this text, stating that Romanian soldiers two years ago did well to go to Great Britain and bring amphorae of olive oil and wine.
But then, two consequences must be drawn from this.

It is not very reasonable symbolically to locate the Food Agency in Helsinki, and it is unreasonable for the European Commission not to backtrack on the following points: first, restore support for almond trees; second, stop

The attacks carried out against olive trees, particularly in Andalusia and throughout Europe, stop the attacks being made against red tuna fishermen in the Mediterranean and the attacks being made against the European vineyards through the rachat.
I suggest our Commissioner, our President, Mr. Barroso, organize a grand banquet in Portugal featuring sheep's cheese and wine.
Mr. President, we have indeed all campaigned for the closure of Guantanamo, and we have all welcomed President Obama's decision to close this prison of shame.
Today, the question of accommodating Guantanamo detainees should not arise for us.
As Europeans committed to our values of defending human rights, we must assume our responsibilities, for it is our duty to welcome these former detainees.
One could read, and I am addressing this to the Council, in the press that some Member States were not very enthusiastic about this reception.
Here, I would like to issue a genuine appeal to the Council, and in particular to the hesitant Member States, urging them to accept hosting these detainees on their territory.

And I would say to you, dear colleagues, that the worst outcome would be for the promising momentum generated by the closure of Guantánamo to falter simply because we failed to respond when Europe was called upon.

This is important, as the European animal feed sector constitutes one of the most significant agricultural sectors, both in terms of production—representing half of the European Union’s agricultural output, some 120 million tonnes—and turnover, amounting to approximately 50 billion euros.
The European Union indeed has five million farmers and sixty million households owning pets.

In the past, the European Union has had to manage a number of health crises, which means that today we must be more vigilant regarding transparency in order to meet the expectations of farmers, as well as those of consumers.

The provisions outlined in this report have the advantage of imposing minimal cost on industries while delivering substantial benefits to consumers, who are increasingly attentive to the quality of the products they purchase.

Adopting this report will help mitigate risks by ensuring better product quality, improved control, enhanced traceability, and greater information availability for the farmer, and ultimately for the consumer.
Today, with the growing volume of international exchanges, it is more than imperative to strengthen every preventive measure so that the food crises we have experienced in the past do not recur.

Mr. President, this report succeeds in balancing the right to information, while strictly defining the nutritional elements that must necessarily appear on labels, with the right to intellectual property, which is so crucial for preserving the competitiveness of our industries.

Mr. President, I believe that manufacturers must indeed be required to immediately notify the use of any new raw material entering into the composition of animal feed, in order to ensure transparency and facilitate oversight by the competent authorities.
The inclusion of an emergency procedure enabling the addition of a new hazardous substance to the list of prohibited substances seems entirely indispensable to me.

Likewise, enabling the producer to consult the competent national authority or the European Commission in case of doubt regarding an unfair claim allows for greater diversification of systems, protects the customer, and maintains the integrity of commercial transactions.

I therefore wish to offer my full support to this excellent report, as experience shows that regulations on animal feed labeling—Mr. President, I conclude—prioritizing quality, transparency, traceability, and control, are the best guarantee for preventing future health crises in Europe.

Mr. President, Ladies and Gentlemen, it is certainly not Europe, nor even its High Representative for Foreign Policy, Javier Solana—former Secretary General of NATO—who can play the role of mediator between Israel and the Palestinians.
At most, they are called upon to finance the reconstruction of the Gaza Strip, as they currently do for Kosovo, Lebanon, or Afghanistan.
Americans and Israelis bomb, Europeans finance reconstruction.
This is the division of tasks among the allies.
Otherwise, it would have to be the vandals who pay.
Egypt is at the center of peace negotiations concerning a prolonged ceasefire with Hamas.

Yet the challenge ahead is formidable, inasmuch as the new Israeli government, under pressure from the third party—Lieberman, leader of an extreme right-wing faction accepted there democratically—may find its task particularly complicated in these negotiations.
Indeed, Netanyahu, who is presented as the future Prime Minister, has consistently opposed a ceasefire with Hamas.
Another difficulty is that Mahmoud Abbas's Palestinian authority has become in the West Bank a kind of international protectorate whose legitimacy has significantly declined among the population.
The last factor to consider is the ongoing expansion of Israeli settlements, which has never ceased since 1967, making the establishment of a Palestinian state in the West Bank particularly delicate.
Today, the ball is in Israel's court, but do the hawks on both sides truly accept this divine ceasefire that both parties demand without taking the necessary steps to achieve it?
Allow me to add a reflection on France's reintegration into the integrated military structure of NATO, which will come up for discussion with the Vatanen report.
This readmission will impose strict obligations on France.
Indeed, we are rejoining NATO at a time when the Cold War has been over for 90 years.
Sarkozy seems to have forgotten the fall of the Berlin Wall and Russia's return to the ranks of free nations.
Should the logic of blocs be reinforced at a time when multipolarity is increasing and emerging countries are gaining strength, including militarily?
Moreover, France's membership in the integrated structure will oblige it to increase its troop presence in Afghanistan, although it already has three personnel on the ground.
At what budget will it then be financed, given that its defense budget will fall below 2% of GDP and that more than 30 regiments will be abolished?
Paradoxically, we will increase our budgetary involvement to join NATO while simultaneously reducing our military presence in Africa.
European defense, dear to President Sarkozy, will therefore be a cornerstone of the Atlantic Alliance.
One needed only to read the Treaty of Lisbon and its additional protocols to be convinced.
Whether in foreign policy or common security, the European path is a dead end that will lead only to alignment with the Americans and their allies.
It is this logic of erasure that we reject in the name of national sovereignty and independence, particularly grounded in our own autonomous nuclear deterrent.
Madam President, I congratulate our three rapporteurs for this rather remarkable synthesis they have produced of the current state of our common foreign and security policy.

Mr. High Representative, you know better than anyone else that Europe is called upon today on the world stage to assume the role assigned to it by its economic and demographic strength, and by the richness of its democratic and humanistic values.

Forced to acknowledge that, despite the consistent willingness demonstrated by more than two-thirds of our European fellow citizens—something you yourself have stated—certain progress has indeed been made over the past ten years, I concede that; yet forced to recognize that this Europe still does not exist.
If further illustration were needed, his disappearance from the solution to the new crisis in the Middle East would provide it.
There has been, and there always will be, a need for a European presence in Gaza to play an active role, both in assisting the survival of the population and in the reconstruction of
country and in the fight against arms smuggling, which had enabled this territory to be transformed into a launch base for rockets of all calibers.
Despite the favorable statements made in support of Charmélcher and Jerusalem, nothing has yet been done in this regard.

Mr. President, I would like to reiterate a question previously raised during the Lebanese crisis: when, Mr. High Representative, can we expect the deployment of a European fleet in the Mediterranean, as we did in response to piracy?
We have the means to do so.
Will we ever have the will for it?
Mr. Commissioner, Mr. President, dear colleagues, the Parliament's decision to accept the urgency requested by the Council regarding the resolution of this matter has not been taken.
The handling of this file has been unanimous, both last week at the Fisheries Committee and this morning, here in plenary session.
We are all indeed aware of the need for the European Union to honor the commitments made in Marrakesh last November by the competent international body, the CICTA.
In concrete terms, this involves implementing the recommendations adopted by this organization in order to address the persistent problem of overfishing of bluefin tuna in the Atlantic.
the Eastern Atlantic and in the Mediterranean, and to confront the serious threat arising from this for the future of the species and thus for fishing itself.
Two of our colleagues, the Fisheries Committee, Mrs. Fraga, and Mr. Romeva i Rueda, were present in Marrakech and will address the matter during this debate.
I would like, for my part, to emphasize the importance our committee attaches to this file, which involves the key dimensions of the common fisheries policy, namely management
on the resource, fleet management, compliance with international, regional, and bilateral agreements, technical measures, and especially monitoring—I will return to this shortly.
This characteristic file therefore, in our view, serves as a test of the credibility of this common fisheries policy.
For this reason, it was obviously out of the question that the implementation of the new recovery plan proceed without consulting the European Parliament.
I therefore welcome the Commission's ultimate decision to pursue the only legally and politically acceptable course for implementing the agreement.
recommendations of the CETA, namely that of a properly drafted regulation proposal based on Article 37 of the Treaty.

The transposition into Community law of commitments undertaken by the Commission on behalf of the European Union within regional fisheries organizations is by no means a trivial matter, and we insist that it always be carried out under the democratic oversight of our institution.
Substantively, I welcome the various measures proposed, which, although binding for our operators, are commensurate with the challenges at hand, and I wish to emphasize the following key ones.
The most important measures are undoubtedly those relating to monitoring, as it is true that without monitoring, no rehabilitation plan can be effective.
I therefore thank you, Mr. Commissioner, for this proposal, and I invite you to show equal delicacy.
determination in implementing the plan concretely, once it has been endorsed by the Council, which you have demonstrated in reaching a satisfactory agreement at the level of the CICTA.
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, I regret that the Council and the Commission have not yet taken concrete steps to define territorial cohesion.
The French presidency is being tried there, and I wish to commend this initiative.
Our Parliament continues to insist that this objective be applicable as of the entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon, and that its details be clarified as soon as possible.

I wish, like the excellent report by Mr. Van Nistelrooij, that territorial cohesion become the legal basis ensuring the balanced development of all territories of the Union and enabling the optimal utilization of the specific characteristics of each European region.
It is essential to better coordinate community policies in order to maximize the impact of on-the-ground interventions.
Territorial cohesion is not intended to target exclusively regions affected by permanent disadvantages.
It must nevertheless focus on the polycentric development of the European Union as a whole, taking into account the specific characteristics of each territory while preserving their diversity.
This new concept appears relevant for addressing the outermost regions in order to ensure their sustainable and balanced growth.

In this regard, I thank the rapporteur for having included in his compromise amendments my requests concerning the specific challenges faced by the ultraperipheral regions in terms of accessibility and competitiveness—essential elements for territorial cohesion.
Upon reading the Commission's recent communication entitled "RUP: An Asset for Europe," it appears to me that it intends to apply to the ultra-periphery the entire
recommendations regarding the improvement of governance in cohesion matters, thus providing a first, somewhat anticipatory, illustration of the implementation of territorial cohesion.

I wish that the overseas general assembly, which will soon begin in France, proceeds in the same manner and extensively incorporates the major territorial impact of European policies supporting the outermost regions, whose added value is undeniable.
irrevocable and undisputed.
One enjoys reading the conclusions of the presidency of the last Council.
It resembles the victory statement issued by every army in retreat.
They have the absurdity of repeatedly asserting that, once the crisis ends, the Union will build a stronger economy for the future, or of reaffirming a Lisbon Strategy in full retreat.
Thus, as they confine themselves within their glass tower, eurocrats, europhiles, and all manner of enthusiasts for Europe ultimately cease to grasp the true measure of things.
Everyone knows well that the eurozone was the first to enter recession, having experienced the world's lowest growth rate for eight years.
But it is also the one facing the greatest difficulties in terms of industrial competitiveness and employment conditions.
Above all, it is she who, by the general disarmament of the natural defenses stripped from nations, is the least armed to respond.
In reality, the only merit of this crisis will be to awaken peoples lulled by the comforts of financial foresight and nationalist propaganda.
Only states will possess the legitimate tools necessary for any large-scale action, and to this end, they will have to challenge the constraints imposed by the Union and the euro.

For the French, at any rate, it is becoming increasingly clear that there will be no salvation without France, no France without French politics, and no French politics without national and popular sovereignty.

Madam President, I believe that the issue of distributing food supplies to the most disadvantaged is indeed timely; however, I think that, fundamentally, the Commission's proposal does not address the actual problem.
The issue of co-financing concerns me, and I believe it is rather the question of liberalism that has guided the evaluations of the CAP in recent years and which should be called into question.
Food riots and abrupt fluctuations in food prices are persistent indicators of the limitations of agricultural market liberalization.
This liberalization has always come at the expense of vulnerable populations, vulnerable producers, and fragile territories.

I therefore advocate that policy should reclaim its rightful place, regain control, both at the European and global levels, and that the strategic domain of agriculture and human food, both in quantitative and qualitative terms, should not be subject to the strict laws of the market.
Certainly, there is urgency, and the Zykerski report responds to this urgency, for which I commend it; however, I wish that, in the case of the reflection currently opening on
For the future of the CAP, it should be recalled that the primary objective is firmly to combat hunger and poverty structurally, as we experience them in all Member States and worldwide.

Rural areas are capable of meeting food needs through sufficient and high-quality production; however, will they be provided with the financial and human resources necessary to fulfill this heavy responsibility?
Mr. President, during this period of economic recession, it was important that the European Union continue to demonstrate its solidarity with developing countries.
Recall that in 2007, the European Community and its member states together provided 60 percent of total development aid, making the European Union the world's leading donor.
European development funds enable the fight against poverty while promoting economic development and democracy.

I therefore welcome the fact that, for ACP countries, a budget of 23 billion euros has been allocated for the period 2008 under the tenth EDF, representing almost double the amount provided for the ninth EDF.
The Court of Auditors, in its report, concludes that the accounts of the seventh, eighth, and ninth FEDs are generally accurate, lawful, and regular.
One can only welcome the record levels of contract implementation and payment disbursement for the FED.
However, I also note that further progress must be made to strengthen the control and monitoring framework, as a significant level of errors continues to affect certain transactions.
Likewise, there is too often an excessive risk regarding financial support, and in my opinion, these risks should be better assessed.
On this matter, I share the view that budgetary support should only be provided if the recipient country has the capacity to manage the funds in a transparent, responsible, and efficient manner.

Mr. President, among the priorities we must address in the future, I would like to mention the integration of the EDF into the Community's general budget, and I will conclude here, as this would strengthen the effectiveness and transparency of development aid.

To conclude, Mr. President, I would also like to thank the rapporteur for his excellent report, the representatives of the Court, as well as all the staff of the Commission who have carried out outstanding work on the ground. I naturally regret the absence of the Council, and in particular that of the Czech Presidency.
Madam President, the Parliament and its rapporteur were compelled to reject a poor compromise; they were right to do so.
Our delegation has remained faithful to the position widely adopted during the plenary session, which called for the eventual elimination of all exceptions to the legal working week.
But the truth is that Parliament was the only one eager to move things forward.
The representatives of the European people had ruled well beyond partisan divides so that this archaism known as the opt-out would come to an end.
We were the only ones to propose a genuine compromise text, which the Council of Ministers superbly ignored, just as the Commission did, incidentally.
The Council and the Commission have aligned themselves with those who firmly oppose any progress in workers' rights in Europe.
Mr. President of the Council, it is clear that you have achieved your objectives.
The Court of Justice obliges you to regard all detention time as working time, so you have lost nothing in this regard.
The opt-out you want still exists in practice, so real estate has prevailed.
More than ever, the deputies elected in June will have to fight to move toward harmonization of social standards from the top down.
Ah! But it's true!

Mr. President, this is certainly not in the name of the LIBE Committee, but most likely in the name of my group. I would like to take this brief moment to express my exceptionally positive assessment of Mrs. Paganelli's report.
I congratulate you, Madam, on your report.

I believe that, in writing this report, you have developed an extraordinarily precise set of requirements for elements that should be prominently included, Mr. Commissioner, in the Stockholm Programme, which I know the Commission is actively preparing.

Allow me to add, regarding the importance of judicial training and evaluation, an emphasis on two fundamental elements of mutual trust, which lie at the foundation of what could become mutual recognition in the future.
One, the independence of the judiciary.
At present, within the Union, there are several countries where the independence of the judiciary from political power or from other powers has not yet been established.
It is a scandal, and this scandal must end.
Secondly, procedural safeguards.

As long as we cannot be certain that, in a number of countries, individuals suspected or accused of committing certain types of offences enjoy procedural safeguards similar to those existing in other countries, it will be difficult to achieve broad acceptance of the principle of mutual recognition.
It is a fundamental aspect, and I wanted to introduce it into this discussion.
Congratulations to Madame Pagat.
Madam President, we cannot remain silent about the situation in Madagascar, and we will not be the only ones to condemn it.
The African Union, the Southern African Development Community, the International Organization of La Francophonie, the Inter-Parliamentary Union, the European Union, the Commission speaking,

In the United States, a large number of countries, including my own and Norway, to name just a few from continental Europe, have condemned the coup d'état, because one must call a spade a spade—it took place in Madagascar.
We cannot remain silent, and we call for a return to constitutional legality.

We never demand anything other than a return to the original distribution of shares, with, if necessary, an arbitration by the Malagasy people through a consultation in the form of a presidential election or a referendum.
It is up to Madagascar's assemblies and political leaders to determine the most effective form of consultation.

Therefore, what we are requesting through this joint resolution is to add our voice to that of the international community in clearly signaling to those who have seized power in a completely unauthorized manner

in a democratic manner, and in a way—however well-disguised, like a coup d'état—to restore Madagascar's constitutional legality, which is one of the guarantees for the future development of this great island in the Indian Ocean.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, of course I would like to thank Commissioner Dimas for his presence and his responses, and for the attention he continues to give to all these disasters.
We worked closely together when President Barroso asked me to prepare a report, which he subsequently cited, on the establishment of a European civil protection force.
I presented this report on May 9, 2006.
When I reread it and see all the disasters that have occurred, I realize it remains ever relevant.
Madame Zavala, of course, there is more than just Greece.
All countries are affected, and I would add that there are not only natural disasters, but also human-made disasters caused by people.
I think of maritime disasters, the Érica and countless others, of industrial accidents such as Chernobyl.
There are other natural disasters besides fires.
I am thinking of the tsunami.
We may one day fear a tsunami in the Mediterranean, as occurred at the beginning of the 20th century, which completely destroyed the city of Messina.

Frankly, Commissioner, my dear colleagues, the tools we are employing are not commensurate with these disasters, which will increase in number under the dual impact of climate change and transportation.
That is why I believe we must be more ambitious.
I thank the Commission, Commissioner Dimas, all their teams, and the Directorate-General for operating the Civil Protection mechanism.
I believe we need to go further.
With member countries that wish to do so, enhanced cooperation can be envisaged.
If we cannot muster twenty-seven to carry out this civil protection force, let us begin with twelve or fifteen.
And then you will see that it will concern everyone.
When I make a list of disasters, I can say that everyone is affected—floods that struck Germany in 2002, major pandemics, and even terrorism.
Here you are, Commissioner, the reason why I would like the Commission to take the initiative to go further, and to propose to the Member States that they go further as well.
Our Parliament, the Environment Committee, and the Defense and Security Committee will support you in these proactive initiatives.

Mr. President, Commissioner, Ladies and Gentlemen Members of Parliament, once again this summer, the Mediterranean forest has been devastated by flames in Marseille, in Southern Corsica, as well as in Italy, Spain, and on a larger scale, in Greece.
More than 400 hectares of forests and fields went up in flames, causing substantial material damage, irreversible harm to biodiversity, and dramatic loss of human lives.
Appalled by this repeated annual toll and aware of the multiplying effect of climate change on wildfires,
I call for a European awareness to improve the coordination of efforts and the pooling of resources for fighting wildfires.
I wish for the realization of a large-scale Euromediterranean cooperation project to combat forest fires within the framework of the Union for the Mediterranean.
I welcome the existing initiatives, and in particular the European Forest Fire Information System, a pilot project.
I nevertheless call for a reform of the European Union Solidarity Fund so that it becomes a high-performing tool for responding to crises and a strengthening of European resilience.
protection and the Euro Aid mechanism conceived by Michel Barnier as early as 2006, designed to operate across the territory of the twenty-seven member countries as well as abroad.

I believe one of the keys to addressing the problem of forest fires lies in the exchange of knowledge and best practices regarding prevention, and I would like to highlight the pioneering role played by forest firefighter personnel in my own department.
I can of course make available to Europe this example, which may prove useful.
Mr. President, Madam President, Madam Commissioner, I will be brief.
I believe there are two elements in this debate.
The conjunctural question: how to get out of this crisis?
All measures that simultaneously assist farmers or constitute intervention measures are necessary and absolutely necessary.
They must be supported, and even considered more significant.
This is what we are proposing, together with a number of colleagues here in Parliament.
Secondly, there is a structurally posed question regarding the management of the milk market, and it is here that we diverge, Madam Commissioner.
You suggest moving towards contractualization.

I am telling you in advance that, by establishing contracts between industry and farmers, and given that industries will be competitive with one another, this will inevitably lead to competition on a European scale.
We need, in order to regulate a market, a public regulation.
There is no other alternative.
It is here that the debate will have to take place following this crisis.
I believe that, on this issue, we must respect all opinions and all options, and I fear we may have moved too quickly at the time of the health check to resolve a matter such as quotas.

Thus far, I say this to all those who have spoken: we have simultaneously managed to maintain dairy production in Europe, sustain an extremely developed dairy industry, and, at the same time, ensure consumer prices that have so far remained entirely affordable.
Mr. President, just a few days after the positive referendum result in Ireland, I truly feel that we can say today that we are more European than we were before.
I truly, fully, and calmly look forward to it.
Unfortunately, unfortunately, we remain essentially dependent on the decision of the Constitutional Court, as well as on the goodwill of the Czech President.
We must send a clear signal of accountability to those who hold in their hands the future of this pivotal treaty for the Union, for our institutions, and for the 500 million Europeans we represent.
We can no longer be hostages to a few individuals whose sole aim is to undermine the general European interest.

While it is legitimate for some to wish to go no further in European integration, and while it is legitimate to express such reservations, it is equally legitimate to allow those who wish it to move forward.
In general, the processes of ratifying European treaties too often resemble sagas or victory dances.
We are condemned to constantly having to exert pressure to ensure the implementation of a treaty and its institutional reforms.
We must draw lessons from this chaotic ratification process, as well as, of course, from this commitment to negotiation aimed at organizing a second

I would like a referendum in Ireland, as if it were normal to make a people vote again after they had sovereignly expressed their will, and as if it were normal for consent to a treaty could be used as a bargaining chip.
It is a matter of the coherence and institutional balance of the European venture.
Ratifying a treaty is a minimal requirement of loyalty and cohesion once one has chosen for one's country to join the European Union.
We wanted to send a strong signal to citizens by ratifying the treaty for the June 2009 European elections.
What do you think today's citizen thinks of the Treaty of Lisbon, and beyond our internal coherence and legitimacy?
Mr. President, my intervention concerns the deterioration of working conditions in Europe.
Today, nearly half of the workers worldwide earn less than two dollars a day.
12.3 million people are still subjected to slavery, and more than 200 million children are forced to work.
In Europe, the number of working poor increases day by day, and the International Labour Organization records 160 deaths annually due to lack of prevention.
In this context, I would like to emphasize our responsibility toward all those who are unemployed and deprived of social rights, who work under degrading conditions.
of these millions of workers who suffer mentally and physically, sometimes driven to suicide or victims of cancers or chronic illnesses due to their working conditions.
It is time to move beyond the religion of working more, the pursuit of profit, and short-term competition.
The right to decent work and the strengthening of the right to work must be top priorities for the Union over the next ten years.
Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, a challenge, a challenge for humanity, a challenge for future generations.

To meet the challenge, Copenhagen must enable a comprehensive, single agreement, as our Environment Committee chair, Joleen Anoui, just emphasized. A coalition of all countries—industrialized and emerging alike—is essential.

Today, through our debate and the adoption of a resolution by our Parliament, we once again call on the European Union to maintain its leadership role in this essential policy of combating climate change.
Let us recall that we must speak with one voice in order to maintain our credibility.
Yes, we must reach an agreement in Copenhagen to limit the increase in global average temperature to a level that does not exceed two degrees above pre-industrial levels.
Yes, we must, in Copenhagen, sign an agreement aimed at achieving collective reductions in greenhouse gas emissions of 30 percent by 2020 compared to 1990.
But this will not be enough.
It is not only necessary for developed countries to significantly reduce their emissions, but also essential for developing countries to contribute to achieving these goals.

Industrialized countries must therefore provide sufficient, sustainable, and predictable financial and technical support to developing countries to encourage them to commit to reducing their greenhouse gas emissions.
Copenhagen must therefore enable developing countries to transition toward low-carbon models.
You too, allow me to address two paths that appear necessary for our future.
First, the establishment at the global level of climate change mitigation financing based on taxing financial transactions.

Subsequently, it seems necessary to implement a border tax adjustment on Europe's borders, targeting the importation of products manufactured without the slightest regard for environmental protection.

This involves contracting and transferring the carbon tax collected at Europe's borders to Southern countries, which would be used to finance investments in the infrastructure necessary to combat global warming.

Mr. President, I would like to speak along the same lines as my intervention yesterday regarding the preparation for the Copenhagen summit, and further in light of yesterday’s ECOFIN meeting, which was not successful.
I have addressed to you the responsibility we bear, as industrialized nations, to ensure that developing countries can keep pace with our efforts to combat climate change.
In Copenhagen, we cannot pretend that we all have the same capabilities; otherwise, it would mean that we all start from the same point and ultimately achieve nothing.
Therefore, to persuade our development partners to join us, we must obviously address the issue of the aid they must receive, and not wait for the outcome of the Copenhagen summit.
At the next European Council, the twenty-seven must absolutely agree on how the European Union envisages providing aid to developing countries within the significant context of climate change.
As I mentioned yesterday, the Council and the Finance Council were unable to reach a common position on this issue.
Yet, we know that this question is absolutely central.

The European Commission has already made proposals; I absolutely wish for us to take the initiative in guiding our partners toward the global solidarity necessary to overcome the challenge of climate change.
Mr. President, I commend the Swedish Presidency for its efforts to achieve an ambitious agreement in Copenhagen, despite the strong winds of pessimism blowing over these forthcoming negotiations.
In my view, it is not realistic to admit at this stage that the Copenhagen summit is already dead or that it will not lead to a comprehensive agreement to be ratified by all parties.
Let us not yield too readily to pessimism.
I advocate that at this stage, we should not lower our ambitions, either in terms of objectives or deadlines.
We must continue to believe in a comprehensive, ambitious, and binding global political agreement, paving the way for the earliest possible conclusion of a legal treaty.
It is truly too early to discuss the deadline for COP 16 in December 2010.
We must now translate our rhetoric into genuine political will.
We must clarify our commitment in financial terms and in providing assistance to developing countries, particularly in terms of technology transfer.
At this stage, a total and collective commitment from the European Union is indispensable.

I further note on paper the recent and exemplary initiative taken by countries such as Brazil, South Korea, Indonesia, Norway, among others, which have quantified their ambitions to reduce polluting emissions.

While one may understand the flexibility requested by the United States, which has only just begun to address climate issues, we must nevertheless achieve specific, binding, and ambitious commitments from other major polluters—such as the United States and China—over short, medium, and long-term periods.
Otherwise, we head straight for climatic, political, and moral disaster.
Madam President, we are all aware, and we have all stated, the importance of the Copenhagen negotiations; but while we all hope for an agreement, we cannot accept an agreement at any price.
If the conditions for an ambitious agreement are not met, I clearly wish for the European Union to be able to say no and refuse to sign a substandard agreement.
The European populations we represent here are awaiting an agreement, an agreement that would enable us to effectively combat the full range of climate disruptions we observe around us every day.
They will not be satisfied with mere announcements, non-specific statements of intent, or non-binding objectives that can be easily disregarded once the media spotlight fades.

In Copenhagen, states will need to commit. The European Union, the only continent to have seen its CO2 emissions decrease since 1990 and the only continent to propose specific, binding, and ambitious commitments for the future, cannot bear the burden of the effort alone.

This is why, if other industrialized and emerging countries are unwilling to assume their share of the burden, we must draw all necessary consequences by imposing at our borders a tax safeguarding our industry against competition from those who choose not to participate in a global agreement.
We, Europeans, increasingly demand more each day from our industrialists.
For proof, I cite the climate package, which was adopted during the French presidency.

For proof, I cite national initiatives, such as the carbon tax recently praised by Mr. Pargno, and for which I likewise express my satisfaction, that promote the establishment of an environmental tax system.

A sector such as transportation, which is among the major emitters of CO2, contributes increasingly to the fight against climate change, even though certain modes of transport, such as aviation, emit fewer greenhouse gases.
Indeed, this is a sector that has been severely affected by the repercussions of the crisis.

If, as I hope, maritime and air transport are included in the Copenhagen negotiations, then let us ensure that whatever is required of European industries is also required of those in other industrialized countries.
In Copenhagen, the world's public opinion will be focused on your leadership class. Please conclude, thank you. Do not disappoint us, I kindly request.
Madam President, dear colleagues, the Copenhagen summit is a historic opportunity to change the course of the planet.
We must reach decisions even more binding than those adopted in Kyoto.
We too must call on our leaders to establish an international organization such as the WTO, entrusted with regulating issues relating to climate change and the environment, and to create a fund.
climate change adaptation, in addition, of course, to public development aid, establishing a maritime and aviation carbon tax, as we must also do for financial transactions.
We clearly see the pressing need for global regulation of the environment, just as for financial markets and the Internet.
We have the opportunity to begin this march of history with the environment.
Let us do it in the image of our highest human aspirations, otherwise we shall appear as leaders with no grasp of the realities of life.

Madam President, Mr. President of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, dear colleagues, less than two weeks before the Copenhagen conference, concerns are growing regarding the success of the climate change negotiations.
Some others are already saying it will be a failure.
Major global powers still hesitate to demonstrate the full ambition that the climate emergency demands.

By adopting tomorrow's resolution proposal on the European Union's strategy ahead of the Copenhagen conference, we, European parliamentarians, must demonstrate that our determination is absolute, and that the conference must lead to an agreement that is not only precise but above all binding.
Moreover, the binding nature of this agreement, once established, must not be subject to challenge or dispute.

One cannot indefinitely postpone decision-making until tomorrow, otherwise it will be too late. Hence the necessary creation of a global environmental organization, which today appears not only essential but also urgent, as it will be entrusted under the auspices of the United Nations with monitoring the implementation of commitments made in Copenhagen.
From Copenhagen onwards, we must be able to expect greater clarity and responsibility from the major players in this world.
Mr. President, I would like to express my support for the joint initiative by the British Labour government and the French government to return undocumented Afghans to their home countries.

Indeed, contrary to what some people have claimed, these individuals had already had their political asylum applications rejected, and this bilateral initiative must now be extended at the European level.

Migration pressure is a challenge we must collectively address, and that is precisely the purpose of the migration and asylum pact, voted on a few months ago and adopted a few months ago under French presidency.
We must now go further by pooling our resources and organizing return flights jointly, funded by Frontex.

The decision by the twenty-seven to request the Commission to conduct a study on this issue is a first step, and, Mr. Commissioner, I wish the Commission would support this proposal, as combating illegal immigration is a public expectation, particularly among regular immigrant populations.
Mr. President, I would like to return to a recent event—the Swiss vote on minarets.
Certainly, this vote took place in a state not a member of the European Union, but it is clear that it touches on an issue that is not foreign to us.
I would like to return to this event, as I also see it as an opportunity to discuss secularism, and we never speak enough about secularism within these walls.
It is necessary and urgent that the principle of secularism govern the organization of our society, and I would like to say here some simple things.
First of all, the opposite principle of secularism is not religiosity, nor even spirituality.
The principle contrary to secularism is religious communalism, which establishes rules and laws superior to civil laws, the communalism that confines individuals within sub-societal groups.
Our Union, by virtue of its history, is multicultural and multiethnic.
She is, and she will increasingly be so, and only secularism can enable individuals
Mr. President, the CESAR programme, which aims to modernize air traffic management in Europe, is a major initiative.

How will the Commission prepare the next phase of the redeployment of these arts, and in particular the funding of this essential program for the environment, as well as for our scientific and technical influence?
Will the Commission follow the recommendations of the 2007 report, which stated that a high-level qualified individual should oversee the political follow-up of this program?
Indeed, it will require overcoming numerous legal, psychological, and technical obstacles by unifying national airspace and enabling air traffic controllers from different countries to work together.
If Europe is incapable of unifying its skies, how could it possibly build a strong political entity?
Mr. President, Madam Minister, dear colleagues, the importance of the issue of climate change no longer needs to be proven.

These impacts, whether short-, medium-, or long-term, are undeniable, even though today's results from the GESE in a certain national journal are making headlines and undoubtedly strengthen the positions of skeptics.
The Copenhagen summit appeared to the world as a unique opportunity for global leaders to bear witness, take action, and address the consequences of the phenomenon known as climate change.

The European Union was at the forefront of this platform, not only because of the bold legislation we have in place regarding the limitation of our activities' impact on the climate, but also because Europe represents a union of countries that can genuinely make a difference.
Certainly, the outcome of the Copenhagen conference can only leave a taste of dissatisfaction, but I refuse to speak of failure.
The world has reached agreement on the issue of limiting temperature rise to two degrees, even among countries traditionally reluctant, such as China.
Brazil has just enacted a law aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions by nearly 39 percent by 2020, in accordance with its commitments.
Many others have also expressed significant ambitions regarding the objectives for reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
Then I think of Japan and Norway.
Moreover, it is also worth highlighting this very positive aspect: the consensus achieved regarding the urgency of establishing a mechanism to reduce emissions resulting from deforestation.
A specific and unanimous text has been established regarding the implementation of the IRED Plus mechanism to reduce deforestation and thereby enable natural carbon dioxide storage.
Far from a binding agreement clearly in line with a political accord, the outcome of Copenhagen must lead us to question the reasons for such disappointment.
I attended the Copenhagen conference, like many of my colleagues, with the intention of participating, contributing, and witnessing a negotiating Europe, a leading Europe in action.
My disappointment was great, as Europe clearly did not receive the place it deserved in the negotiations, and all the more so when it comes to the European Parliament.
To assert itself, Europe must demonstrate greater strength on this issue.
The meeting of our ministers on the environment in Seville did not lead anywhere, and our officials have appeared uncertain, hesitant, and we remain divided on our objectives.
I hope that, drawing on her proposals and commitments made before the Committee on the Environment, Mrs. Heidegger will succeed in raising the ambitions of the European Union to an appropriate level.

Mr. President, dear colleagues, in order to move beyond caricatures—some of which we heard this morning—I would like to recall a few objective facts regarding Tunisia, a partner country of the European Union.

Social conditions have improved significantly, marked by a spectacular rise in average income per capita, and today the vast majority of Tunisians meet the criteria defining membership in the middle class.

Over 90 percent of Tunisians benefit from social coverage, and more than a quarter of Tunisia's budget is allocated to education, placing the country among the first to achieve the Millennium Development Goals for primary education.
Finally, Mr. Michel reminded us just now that women's rights are recognized and guaranteed.
At the university, there are more female students than male students; 40 of the professors are women; one quarter of the deputies, local officials, or journalists are women.
Many countries could envy such results.
Mr. President, the acts of criminal fanatics who killed Christians in Egypt cannot be attributed to an entire people and its government.
It would be unjust to hold Egypt and the Egyptians responsible for this abominable massacre, the perpetrators of which will be brought to trial.
Let us not confuse the actions of a criminal with the policy of a country, nor equate fanatics with an entire population. Finally, by claiming to interpose ourselves
Between the Coptic Christians of Egypt and their Muslim compatriots, we provide arguments to extremists seeking to portray Eastern Christians as agents of the West.
I have heard you; excuse me, Madam President, Madam President, Ladies and Gentlemen, what is happening in Greece, in Portugal, tomorrow in Spain or Ireland is scandalous.

These same Anglo-Saxon agencies, which complacently rated the rogue issuers and traders of toxic assets and failed to foresee the crisis, are now displaying the utmost severity toward states that stepped in to rescue a financial sector that did not deserve such assistance.

The crisis caused by the irresponsibility of markets and banks, along with this massive assistance, is directly responsible for the worsening of budget deficits and public debt, which these same markets now claim to penalize.
This is what the moralization of capitalism, proclaimed by Mr. Sarkozy, the European Union, and the G20, has come to.

All that bankers have taken away from the crisis is that taxpayers are an inexhaustible source of profit and insurance, since it is widely believed that the scale of this panic originated with a major American bank that received government assistance.

of the American state and two American hedge funds seeking to profit simultaneously from the exorbitant interest rates imposed on Greece and from the CDS market, these insurance contracts on sovereign debt that in themselves are the subject of independent speculation.
It will not be enough to regulate hedge funds, which you fear to do, or the markets for derivative instruments, as Mr. Barnier proposes.
It is pointless to create a European economic government.
The twenty-seven are in the same boat anyway, and solidarity is not an option.
It is the global free circulation of capital that must be called into question, otherwise there will be no
Mr. President, less than 15% of the original stock of red wine remains.
Faced with such figures, the solution appears obvious.
That said, we must not forget the jobs affected by the decision before us today.
It should be recalled that CITES is not intended to ban fishing, but solely to regulate the international trade of this fish, 80% of which is exported to Japan.

By safeguarding tuna, we not only protect a species dangerously overfished, but also promote a more balanced and sustainable fishing activity—fishing aimed at our domestic market and capable of generating employment.

Mr. President, I am in favor of including the red tuna in Appendix 1 of CITES, and although I believe this decision will be positive, I call on the Commission to support a thorough restructuring of the fishing sector.
My question therefore concerns the terms of this support.
France requests an 18-month postponement, accompanied by financial measures for mariners and shipowners affected by a trade ban.
What is the Commission's vision on this matter?
Moreover, my concern, like that of many of my colleagues, is fairness.
How can we ensure that ships flying the flags of countries such as Tunisia, Libya, or others will enforce the ban on international trade with a level of rigor comparable to our own?
What will the new measures for monitoring and sanctions be?

Our policy on the sustainability of economic activities must be concrete, and at times even unpopular in certain sectors, but I hope the Commission and the Council do not lose sight of the necessary adjustments required for implementing these measures.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, as the rapporteur has reported, this proposal for a directive indeed follows upon a vote by Parliament, a point you have also recalled, Mr. Commissioner.
Yet, my group remains highly critical of this proposal because it risks backfiring against those we aim to support.
By seeking to reduce the burdens on businesses, we risk depriving them of the conditions of transparency and trust essential to their management and the vitality of their operations.
