First of all, I believe it is essential to be clear about the thresholds and about what is referred to as micro-entities.
These are SMEs with more than ten employees and annual turnover exceeding one million euros.
Regardless of the member states, they represent a very large majority of enterprises.
Certainly, they may be local and have limited presence on the community market, but this does not diminish the fact that the need to establish accounting rules will remain for them and that
Accounting rules will confront them each time they wish to deal with a banking institution, with partners, or occasionally negotiate with fiscal, economic, and social institutions.
At that point, accounting rules will be imposed upon them, and they may have to pay more than for regular accounting in order to meet criteria they may not necessarily master.
I do not see any progress in imposing accounting requirements outside of any common framework within the European Union.
This is not merely a matter of competition; it is fundamentally a question of economic integration and community law, as well as equal treatment for all enterprises throughout the European Union.
That said, of course, simplifying accounting obligations for SMEs is a necessity.
Three other initiatives also move in this direction, whether it be the proposal we are discussing today, or the international IF framework.
I prefer that it be the revision of the fourth and seventh accounting directives, for which the European Commission should soon present us with proposals.
These proposals were promised to us for 2010; it appears they are being extended, but I do not believe this extension of deadline is a sufficient reason for this
rush into things and do half a job, as these three proposals, even though they share the same objective, may not be articulated in the same way, and I believe it would be better, Mr. Commissioner,

It is clearer for businesses to provide them with a single, comprehensive response that will enable them to balance the shared goal of simplifying obligations with the practical realities faced by small enterprises that require such support.
The current issue is that the European Commission is delaying its proposal for a revision of this directive, and this delay is detrimental to our deliberations.

I therefore call on the European Commission to accelerate the dialogue on the issue of exemption, which is currently dividing both institutions and European economic circles, and to carry out an impact assessment.

Mr. President, Commissioner, and dear colleagues, as my colleague Elisabeth Morin-Chartier and others have pointed out, the violent storm Xynthia struck several French regions on February 27 and 28, resulting in 53 fatalities and extensive material damage.
The storm was of exceptional intensity, causing devastating floods along the French coastline.
After Madeira, Europe is once again affected.
Brittany, the region of my colleague and friend Alain Cadec, was severely affected.
Three French departments have thus been declared to be in a state of natural disaster: Brittany, Poitou-Charentes, and the Loire regions.
In the face of these disasters, the European Union must demonstrate its responsiveness and express its solidarity.
It is not only through words and a resolution, but above all through financial assistance, that we will be able to support the victims of these disasters.

I therefore invite, and my colleague Christophe Bécuy joins me in this request, the European Commission to swiftly release the Union's Solidarity Fund to assist the affected regions in coping with the damages.
This disaster underscores the relevance of Michel Barnier's proposal to establish a European civilian intervention force.

Europeans must act together, as disasters are increasing and affecting our European territories, particularly our most isolated and vulnerable regions, such as the islands of the Indian Ocean and the Caribbean.

Madam President, I would also like to congratulate our rapporteur, Jürg Leichtfried, for his report and his spirit of cooperation on this matter, and I particularly support his proposals regarding the funding of aviation security, since we are discussing aviation security, not aviation safety.

It has been many years now that our Commission has been advocating for the principle that the strictest security measures must be borne by the Member States, and I fully support our rapporteur on this matter.
Indeed, security concerns not only travelers but all citizens, and it is the responsibility of states to take measures to combat terrorism.
It is therefore unjustified that air passengers alone should bear the cost of security measures at airports and on board aircraft.
And this is all the more so because, in modes of transport such as rail and maritime, states partially or fully fund security measures, depending on the circumstances.
I therefore believe we must remain firm in our stance toward the Council and defend, to the very end, amendment 32 of the report adopted by our Committee on Transport.
I had another point to address briefly.

While I support the progress in non-discrimination and transparency that this text will bring, I do not believe the objective of this directive is to establish a single, uniform European system for security fees.
While ensuring compliance with common rules, Member States must be allowed to maintain their current system for collecting royalties.

Thus, in countries such as Spain, Italy, or France, royalties are approved by Parliament, and in such cases, Parliament must serve as the independent supervisory authority and the body of appeal against laws.
And this is precisely the purpose of Amendment 40, which I hope many of you will support when we vote on it.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, the report I present to Parliament seeks to explain that, in the fight against climate change, agriculture has a role to play and a place to occupy.
This week, nature reminds us that volcanoes in particular can have a major impact on human activities and on the climate.
This nevertheless requires that men, and Europe in particular, take their responsibilities in combating what is known as global warming.
My report attempts to demonstrate that agriculture can simultaneously continue to reduce its carbon emissions, decrease its consumption of fossil energy, and, above all, on another front,
Moncotte has actively contributed to carbon fixation, thereby globally reducing carbon emissions and increasing the amount of carbon fixed and sequestered.
This report attempts to show that in agriculture, we should move away from a somewhat compartmentalized approach—wherever a problem arises, we simply open a new directive.
on the soil, on water, on pesticides, who knows what else, and to attempt to address the agricultural issue with the ambition of treating it in a comprehensive, systematic manner.

In this approach, it must be possible to identify pathways that enable agriculture, as I mentioned earlier, not only to reduce its energy consumption but also to actively contribute to carbon sequestration.
It is particularly the role related to photosynthesis, biomass, and above all what is close to my heart—linked to soil, and to the capacity of European soils to sequester organic carbon.

I believe that this path I am opening in this report—and I hope it will be followed during the vote to be held in plenary in Brussels—must enable European agriculture to enter a phase of sustainability, ensuring a

ecological transition of this agriculture, placing it ahead of numerous other continents or agricultural systems, so that it becomes economically viable, ecologically sound, and, I believe, also socially effective from that point onward.

This report addresses a specific issue—the fight against climate change and agriculture—but it also aspires, I hope, to open up other perspectives to help fuel, in particular, the debate on the future of agricultural policy after 2013.
I am addressing the Commission; I have done so frequently, and I will continue to address the agricultural committees; I will also address the various ministers whenever the opportunity arises.
I think we need to make a turn.
This 2013 CAP must be ambitious.
It must be based on objectives that enable citizens to understand why we need a common agricultural policy.
We need a common agricultural policy, because Europe's agriculture must ensure food security while also committing to sustainability.
This is at least what I propose in this report, and this is what I hope the Parliament, by a broad majority, will follow.

Mr. President, I would first like to emphasize that the precautionary principle implemented by most European governments—closing their airspace in a targeted and temporary manner—was a wise and prudent decision.

The safety of our fellow citizens must take precedence over any other consideration, and under these circumstances, the stance adopted by certain airlines demanding the immediate and total reopening of airspace, even for a single or double test flight, appears at the very least indecent.

I believe it was Mr. Henain earlier who spoke about the complementarity of transport modes, particularly with regard to trains, and I will take this opportunity to express how much I regret the air transport paralysis
has been exacerbated in certain countries, such as France, by the disruption of rail transport due to strikes under these irresponsible and incomprehensible circumstances.
Returning to our subject, I wish to welcome the Commission's decision to authorize the granting of public aid to airline companies affected by the ongoing disruptions.
And this is a sensible decision in a context already marked by crisis, but these measures must be of an exceptional nature.

In this regard, I wish that among the criteria used to grant them, consideration be given to the exemplary conduct demonstrated by the companies in compensating their customers who were affected by flight cancellations.
Indeed, it is unacceptable for certain airlines to abuse the force majeure clause in order to evade their obligation to compensate passengers arising from Regulation 261/2004.
Travelers are victims of the current situation, and if no alternative solution is offered to them, they must not additionally bear the financial burden.
Moreover, travel agencies are exempt from the obligation to refund unused flights to travelers.
And this is not normal either.

Companies, like travel agencies, have insurance coverage for exceptional circumstances such as the one we have been experiencing over the past few days, and it is therefore essential to ensure that travelers are properly compensated for flights that have been cancelled.
Mr. President, chaos, disorder, paralysis, cacophony, catastrophe, disaster—there is no shortage of words in the press to describe the closure of European airspace and its consequences.
I will not dwell on what has already been said, particularly regarding the immense, abyssal, one might even say, financial impact of this crisis, whether expressed directly or indirectly.

While I welcome the possibility of considering exceptional public aid to the aviation sector, already heavily supported since September 11, 2001, I remain nonetheless perplexed by the European handling of these events.

1. Knowing that 750 European passengers were injured, many of whom remain stranded in various corners of the world, and knowing that economic losses grow exponentially with time, how can one explain that it took

It takes not one, two, three, or four, but five days for European transport ministers to convene via teleconference in order to coordinate their actions and decide on the establishment of differentiated traffic zones.
Second, no one disputes the need to apply the precautionary principle with absolute priority, but today, as traffic is gradually being reinstated in certain areas

secured, with unchanged weather conditions, and given that the volcano remains active, we are entitled to question what additional safety assurances for passengers we could not have provided earlier or more quickly.

Thirdly, an evolving action model has been developed; the evolution according to weather conditions and volcanic activity can change hourly, and this model will continue to carry out updated assessments of corridor safety.
Should these test flights be carried out by civil aviation and airlines?

Finally, if it persists, worsens, or recurs—which is likely—the management model implemented will need to provide for greater coordination among Member States and establish special operational procedures based on real-time data.

coordination of other means of transport, under exceptional circumstances, while also taking into account the necessary coordination of assistance for the tens of thousands of stranded passengers, who likewise have the right to information and assistance.
Or, to date, only isolated and national initiatives have been taken.

Madam President, I too, following other speakers, would like to express my gratitude to our colleague Méndez de Vigo, who has carried out outstanding work under sometimes heated, yet always engaging, circumstances.
Debates within the Committee on Professional Affairs lasted a long time, meeting both the gravity of the issue and the intensity of interest clearly demonstrated by the countries concerned.
The European Parliament, whose main issue concerning them in the most legitimate manner possible, is the composition of our assembly and the procedures for appointing members of our assembly.
Once again, the rapporteur has succeeded in bringing together sometimes differing opinions and contributions, and synthesizing them, I would say, into an extremely balanced text.
I wanted to thank him for it.

I believe the recommendations contained in these two reports—both the one concerning the convening of the IGC and the one regarding transitional measures for the composition of our assembly—are marked by realism, pragmatism, and effectiveness.
I believe this is, overall, what citizens also expect from Europe on other issues.
Mr. President, Mr. Dalli, Commissioner, this draft report, as presented just now by Mr. Peterle, strongly reaffirms the Commission's positions.
It is the communication directive from the European Commission and also draws upon the resolution of the European Parliament of 10 April 2008 on the fight against cancer in our European Union.

Allow me to support the objectives of the European partnership envisioned by the European Commission to combat cancer more effectively, including the importance of prevention and early detection, the development of a new model for cancer prevention, and, above all, the reduction of inequalities among member states.
I share the concerns and apprehensions expressed in the European Commission's communication and the draft report.
I would like to commend Mr. Peterle, the rapporteur, for the work accomplished in drafting this report and for the proposed compromise solutions aimed at incorporating the various amendments.

As fictitious rapporteur for the Socialist and Democratic Group, I wished, among other things, to highlight the following issues: first, the increase in annual cancer-related deaths caused by exposure to carcinogenic agents in the workplace, as well as the importance of improved access

information on medicines for cancer patients, as well as the implementation of the REACH regulation and the regular updating of substances of very high concern, including carcinogenic substances, and support for initiatives aimed at preventing the importation of goods

containing carcinogenic chemicals, and to strengthen monitoring of the presence of chemical substances within the European Union, and finally, the development of guidelines for a common definition of disability that may include individuals suffering from chronic illnesses or cancer.
Here are the questions we have raised while supporting this draft report.
Madam President, the spill from the oil well in the Gulf of Mexico is a true ecological and economic catastrophe.
It appears that irregularities may have been detected in the granting of forest permits by the U.S. Department of the Interior.
This would constitute an unforgivable negligence whose consequences are incalculable and which concern not only the United States of America.

Given that hundreds of offshore drilling permits are granted by states around the world, if such permits were issued with the same lack of seriousness, it would pose serious problems for the entire world.
elected representatives from European overseas regions and territories composed entirely or almost entirely of islands, nearly 130
In total, spread across the three continents, I must express here my profound concern regarding our islands in light of offshore oil exploration projects.
This is why it is imperative that the European Union, tasked with setting an example, re-examines with the utmost rigor the safety and prevention measures on its own offshore oil platforms.
Moreover, accidental or operational marine oil spills, not necessarily attributable to Europe, are estimated at several tens of thousands of tonnes annually.

Would it not be appropriate for the European Union to take initiatives so that offshore platforms henceforth fall under an undisputed international scientific authority, which could be established in the form of an international body?

Following Copenhagen and in preparation for Cancun, the European Union would do itself a great honor by initiating genuine global governance for the research, protection, and exploitation of deep-sea resources.

The continuity of the Barcelona Process, the Union for the Mediterranean, presents an opportunity for the development of our relations with the countries of the eastern and southern Mediterranean basin.
As Vincent Peillon's excellent report underscores, we cannot overlook either the potential of this region or the dangers it entails.
Yet, having the clarity to acknowledge this since the bombardment of Gaza and the ongoing colonization of Palestinian territories, the Union for the Mediterranean is at an impasse.
The political component is stalled.
It is not even certain that the summit scheduled for next June in Barcelona will actually take place.
I would not be surprised if we learned in the coming hours or days that he has been postponed, adjourned.
We must not therefore give up, but the European Union must take greater responsibility in seeking a political solution.
We cannot allow the United States to have a monopoly on the political management of the crisis in this region, which is ours, while limiting ourselves to contributing to its development.
The development and stability of the Mediterranean are essential to our security and growth, and for this purpose, the Union for the Mediterranean remains the best instrument at our disposal.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, this is an extremely important issue concerning consumer protection.
A recent survey indicates that Europeans perceive the food security situation as having worsened over the past ten years, and one in three consumers remains skeptical about product quality.
Updating, simplifying, and clarifying food labeling within the European Union is therefore a necessity for better consumer protection.
Allow me to address two points.
Regarding the color code, we support this code provided it is accompanied by a reference to nutritional values expressed as percentages.
Moreover, I am opposed to a mandatory nutritional declaration for wines and spirit drinks.
We wish to establish a regulation specific to wines and spirits within the next three years, rather than the five years proposed by the European Commission.
That is what I wished to point out, Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner.
Mr. President, Madam High Representative, this attack on civilian vessels by Israeli commandos undoubtedly requires an impartial and credible investigation.
But what credibility could we grant to a commission that would be both judge and party?
This attack occurred in international waters, and thus concerns the international community, particularly the European countries bordering the Mediterranean.
Europe, which should speak out more and become more actively involved in seeking a political solution based on two states, each with Jerusalem as its capital.

Europe is making considerable financial efforts to contribute to the development of Palestinian territories, but unfortunately, it is too absent from the political process that should lead to the establishment of a Palestinian state.
Our financial and budgetary goodwill is not enough.
We must also express a political will and take the necessary measures to make ourselves heard.
The brutal murder of the Mavi Marmara by the Israeli army has provoked strong emotions and widespread condemnation.
I would like to express my emotion regarding the nine victims of the civilian assault, condemnation, as this seizure in international waters constitutes a violation of international law.
By doing so, the Israeli government penalizes its own people, as ultimately, with the strengthening of extremist factions, the ultimate goal of security in the region recedes further.
Yes, more than ever, the government of Israel must abandon the politics of force.
I wish to point out, however, that the intense media coverage surrounding the Mavi Marmara flotilla, orchestrated from Turkey, casts doubt on the exclusively humanitarian nature of this operation.
An investigation appears indispensable to shed light on the assault, as well as on the preparations that preceded this expedition.
And I wish that the government of Israel would accept that this investigation include substantial international participation, so that justice may finally be restored in this region.
Madam President, Madam High Representative, I welcome the new momentum generated by the launch of the partnership for modernization and its pragmatism.
This new partnership, even if it achieved nothing else, would at least have the merit of highlighting just how indispensable Russia remains as a geopolitical partner for the European Union.
I also welcome the positive remarks regarding cooperation with Russia on crisis management.
A few remarks, however.

While the requirements of the Union are consistently emphasized, the interests or positions of its partner appear systematically underestimated or disregarded, whether in the field of energy or in the way it assesses the Eastern Partnership and the Atlanticist aspirations of its closest neighbors.

I also note that the firmness displayed by most member states and the Union in their statements, as well as the severity of their condemnations and allegations of human rights violations, appear to be reserved exclusively for Russia.

The remarks are more restrained, diplomacy more filtered, as if referring to China, for instance, regarding these systematic violations of the rule of law—merely in relation to the working conditions at this global factory, when judged against minimal European social standards, especially considering that our French or European workers are unemployed.

Allow me to remind you of Adagusta's saying, father Augusta: "To the peaks by narrow paths," for this is how I view Russia's current efforts, freed from the criminal poison of Marxism that, for so many decades, bled and imprisoned this land.
The current Russian Federation has managed to lift the head of an entire nation, despite the significant difficulties and challenges that still lie ahead.

Therefore, a little modesty, gentlemen of European integration, especially when the super European state itself fails to achieve any of its noble objectives, despite increasingly costly resources and the growing dominance of the Western European and Central and Eastern European states.
I cannot help but recall that this Assembly showed greater indulgence toward the more communist Soviet Union than toward Russia.

Indeed, it is often the same individuals who, yesterday, advocated for the unilateral disarmament of the West and for participation in Mr. Gorbachev's "common house," who now experience the most virtuous indignation and the greatest sense of propriety regarding the Russia-EU partnership.

On this subject, I must inform you—should you not yet be aware—that new historical facts will emerge as a result of the gradual translation of secret Soviet archives, which a courageous individual, Mr. Pavel Stroilov, has managed to obtain, archives that have already yielded and will continue to yield significant revelations.

It is an unwelcome surprise for the chief advocate of orthodox thinking regarding the reality of the end of the Cold War, which was indeed, Mr. Gorbachev, and the betrayal by today’s Europeanist powers, who at the time enthusiastically sought to make certain Soviet projects possible.
At the risk of repeating myself, Russia is an essential and strategic partner for European states, and our relations must be strengthened for the benefit of all parties.

This country is far closer to us in terms of culture, civilization, history, geography, mutual interests, or shared risks than certain states you are nevertheless about to admit to the European Union.
Mr. President of the Council, it is often said that necessity makes law.
Faced with the crisis, an obvious truth, nay, an inevitable necessity—innovation, creativity.

In this regard, I welcome the progress made by the Council—a step I hope will ultimately prove to be a giant leap toward genuine economic governance, for let us not be mistaken: mere coordination of economic policies will remain insufficient.
Could we achieve a genuine budgetary federation, not imposed by circumstances, but because we had collectively decided upon it ourselves?
Let us return to the Council's decisions, which nonetheless please me.
Strengthening the preventive component of the Stability and Growth Pact is a positive development, but there can be no genuine budgetary discipline without sanctions for those who deviate from it.
They have been strengthened, but not yet clarified—that is regrettable.

Otherwise, I shall express my satisfaction, among other things, regarding the decisions taken, such as the renowned competitiveness performance dashboard, since that is precisely what is at stake—reconnecting with competitiveness and sustainable growth for all.
We also commend the proposed introduction of a banking tax, the acceptance of taxing financial transactions, and the acceleration of financial regulatory reform.
Mr. President of the Council, all these decisions and guidelines seemed, just a few months ago, or even just a few weeks ago, unrealistic, illusory, or indeed truly hypothetical.
You're right, it's true, it represents a profound cultural shift on the scale of Europe's incremental policy approach.
That said, it is now essential that, by October, the states concretely implement these guidelines.

It will now be up to the Council whether this achievement constitutes a historic leap forward or merely an empty wish, something we will all bitterly regret, and which a majority—citizens—will particularly have to endure on a daily basis.

Mr. President, we have already discussed this matter during the Presidents' Conference. Many groups remain divided on the Gallo report, and clearly the Parliament as a whole—indeed, all groups—still need to discuss it, as there are contradictory points that the groups have been unable to resolve.
That is why we requested postponing the debate and vote on the Gallo report, namely on September 2, which will require consultation with the Conference of Presidents.
Therefore, it is a very simple matter.
Since the fruit is not yet ripe, let it ripen over the summer and let's discuss it in September.
Madam President, I must admit I find it difficult to follow my colleagues, who so eagerly display their strength and boast of our monopoly on legitimacy vis-à-vis the other institutions of the Union.
Unfortunately, their stance is often laughable.
But when the members of this house, through hard work and conviction, assert a well-reasoned position, when
We defend what resembles a European general interest against rigid states, and the situation changes completely.
In this debate on European supervision, the Parliament has played this role.
Regarding the European Systemic Risk Board, of which I am the shadow rapporteur for my group, I have advocated, like the rapporteur, Ms. Goulard, for an ambitious approach.
I have advocated mechanisms ensuring a certain level of effectiveness, and I have emphasized the primacy of the European Central Bank.
Why?
Because the European Central Bank is indeed the only community institution within this framework, and because it has demonstrated its seriousness.
Out of Christian charity, I feel compelled to withhold the names of all the institutions and eminent figures who suddenly vanished during the storm.
We can consider ourselves fortunate to have had at least one captain on board during the height of the crisis.
I shall therefore be reassured to see tomorrow's macroprudential supervision anchored in a strong community institution, while microprudential supervision will inevitably remain fragmented.
Having become unsettled, the European Systemic Risk Board must get to work as soon as possible.
The future will show whether adjustments will be necessary, but it is essential to start on sound foundations, as systemic risks affect the entire European and global banking sector.
Mr. President, the final agreement reached on the revision of the directives concerning industrial emissions is more than acceptable, and I commend the work of compromise that has been achieved.

This final package incorporates the key tax policy points I have advocated for, alongside several of my colleagues on the Environment Committee, with the aim of making this text an ambitious yet realistic piece of legislation.
First, maintaining a certain degree of flexibility for businesses and competent authorities in granting exemptions.
Next, I would like to welcome the new version of the ESN, so controversial and now limited to certain sectors, which appears much more achievable.

It is also important to emphasize that the agreement reached enables better alignment with the ETS directive and definitively removes from the text of the new directive the somewhat irrelevant notion of establishing a market for SO2 and NOX allowances.
Some caveats to keep in mind, however, particularly the provisions regarding the ground.
Since the SOL Directive remains pending, this important issue will unfortunately be addressed only by those Member States that have their own legislation in place.
Tomorrow, Parliament must adopt the elements of the agreement reached following the trilogues.

Enormous efforts have been made from all sides to prevent this text from being referred back for conciliation—a text that, I must emphasize, is of crucial importance both for industry and for European environmental policy.
Madam President, each day brings new information revealing the extent of the ecological disaster caused in the Gulf of Mexico by the offshore platform rupture.
But let us not forget that other disasters of this nature are occurring elsewhere.
I am thinking in particular of what is happening today and has been happening for a long time already in Nigeria.

That is why, in addition to the moratorium on new drilling, I believe there is a need to promote stricter regulations regarding platform management, not only European rules but also international ones.
As for the European Union, I believe it is necessary for it to proactively establish measures to combat this disaster, and I think it is clear
I also believe that all this, as Ms. Damanaki stated, underscores the need for a firm and resolute choice in favor of clean and renewable energies.
The European Union should set an example in this regard.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, reopening negotiations with Mercosur at a time when the European Union is preparing to redefine its agricultural policy reveals a dangerous lack of coherence on the part of the Commission.
Europe already imports 500 tonnes of beef.
A new opening of our market would come at the expense of farmers in disadvantaged European regions, without assurance of compliance with sanitary and social standards.
We cannot accept this.
Europe purchases 38 million tonnes of soy and livestock feed.
These imports from Brazil and Argentina account for more than 19 million hectares.
They are concentrated in the hands of three companies: Cargill, ADM, and Bunge.
Between 2001 and 2004, soybean production destroyed more than 1.2 million hectares of tropical and equatorial forests.
It was one of the primary causes of the increase in greenhouse gas emissions and the loss of biodiversity.
The European Parliament has decided to produce a report on the recovery of vegetable protein autonomy for livestock feed.
Environmental-friendly agriculture and the fight against climate change dictate this choice.
These efforts will be rendered futile by this proposed bilateral agreement.
Once again, the European Commission is selling out its agriculture and farmers in exchange for uncertain concessions for service companies.
These concessions will in no way benefit the peasant families of Mercosur, but only the interests of agro-industrial groups.
To be convinced, one need only read the report issued by the Tyson group, an American company and major player in the international meat market.
which announces having invested in Brazil, and, to quote directly from its report, to use this country as a platform for exporting to Europe.
Other companies, such as Doo Corporation, had already anticipated this move by acquiring the Brazilian group Frango Soul in 1998.
Many poultry farmers, particularly in Brittany, had suffered losses as a result of this operation.
Europe, without wishing to continue its policy of offshoring, intensifies its attacks.
against the peasants, we must stop
Mr. President, many colleagues are concerned about the potential consequences for European agriculture resulting from the reopening of negotiations on an association agreement with Mercosur.
My group and I share these concerns.

It is indeed to be feared, and as a deputy representing an ultra-peripheral region, I have firsthand experience with banana agreements. It is therefore to be feared that an agreement with Mercosur would deal a severe blow to many European agricultural sectors.
This is why an impact assessment is absolutely necessary.

It is also feared that this agreement may further, and perhaps excessively, orient Mercosur's agriculture toward a productive model, to the detriment of the small-scale farms that exist in these countries.
An equilibrium must therefore be established so that this agreement does not transform the Latin American and European continents into superpowers essentially grounded in the dogma of mercantilism.
Mr. President, I would simply like to say that we may not have properly set today's agenda.
If we allow free trade agreements to bring products into Europe that do not comply with either our social standards or our environmental standards, then the reform of the CAP will be pointless.
Very clearly, we cannot remain in a hypocrisy whereby, on one hand, we impose environmental, sanitary traceability, and safety constraints.
to our farmers, on the grounds that consumers must be protected, and on the other hand, allowing products to enter our continent freely without adhering to these standards.
The issue at stake behind the negotiation with Mercosur is precisely that one.

The way in which matters were initiated, regardless of what we are told about Parliament having been very well informed and about proceedings having taken place with the greatest transparency and clarity, does not lead us to view favorably a negotiation that occurs at a time when negotiations at the WTO are simultaneously ongoing.
On the grounds of consistency, consumer protection, and the defense of our farmers, I do not believe we should continue along this path.

Madam President, I would also like to commend the work carried out by rapporteur José Bové, to acknowledge the Commissioner, and to welcome the intervention of Mr. Deß, who preceded me, on the objectives of this report.

I believe it is very important that we clearly keep in mind that, in the debate we are having, we are addressing a problem related to price volatility in agriculture and especially to the fact that when prices
They quickly affect distribution prices, but when prices decrease at the production level, there is no corresponding reduction for consumers.
There is indeed an organizational issue within the supply chain, and there is indeed a problem regarding the distribution of what is called added value within the supply chain.

I believe José Bové's report offers, on several points, perspectives that must be highlighted, and here I am addressing the Commission directly in an effort to break out of this fundamentally negative spiral.
The first, of course, is the organization of the supply chain and the balance of power within the supply chain.
What proposals do we put forward to enable producers, when gathered and organized, to have greater influence within the supply chain when negotiating?
That is the question you raised, Mr. Commissioner, regarding interprofessional bodies, but it is also a question arising from the contractual framework that will be established.
I argue, and it is within the report that this contractual basis has a European framework.
One cannot allow each country to solve this problem on its own.
There must be a European framework.
It must be flexible, but it must exist.
It is a suggestion, an inseparable path for me.
Secondly, of course, the issue of price transparency.
And on this point, I support the establishment of this observatory, which is absolutely necessary in order to inform consumers about the evolution of prices.
Here, I support the proposals that have been made, and I hope, as Mr. Desch said, that there will be a majority for
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, today I wish to welcome this debate, which once again underscores the need for the Union to assess and value its regional diversity.
During these years of negotiating the new framework programmes for 2014, I believe it is important to emphasize the adjustments that specific regions should benefit from.
Questions of continuity and territorial cohesion must be at the heart of our concerns.
But the development of mountainous, insular, or sparsely populated regions requires more than just a set of derogatory measures.
We need a horizontal approach.
We must establish the tools capable of synergistically integrating all areas of community action in order to enhance their added value.
Moreover, as a representative of the outermost regions, I am eagerly awaiting the new Union strategy for the OCTs, which the Commission's services promised us for early 2011.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, first of all, I join my colleague in expressing concern over the absence of the Council from this meeting of Parliament.
I find that a bit careless on the part of the Council, and I want to say that as well.
As the next summit approaches on October 6, China and the European Union must absolutely and resolutely strive together to make progress on the issue of climate change.
Last year, at the same time, we were already engaged, in this very hemicycle, urging Europe to do everything possible to achieve an ambitious and binding agreement in Copenhagen.
It has been shown that the outcome did not meet our expectations and ambitions.
Therefore, one year later, concerns remain the same, or even considerably more intense.

In order to be able to constrain or convince our partners today, we must demonstrate that our determination remains absolutely intact, for example by showing that we provide the resources necessary to fulfill our commitments to Southern countries.
To this end, the establishment of innovative financing mechanisms has become a necessary imperative—and I heard President Sarkozy's speech at the UN yesterday.
Therefore, for example, the introduction of a carbon tax at our borders cannot be ruled out.

It would act as an incentive mechanism for countries that are not going far enough in their climate commitments, far more so than a mere threat, and serve as a means to protect our jobs and businesses, particularly in better addressing the post-crisis period.

Madam President, the need to enhance family leave is beyond dispute. Raising the minimum threshold for maternity leave is a step forward, a benefit, and we must not be entirely demagogic in comparing its economic impact to the qualitative benefits, which are difficult to quantify.

But the problem is essentially twofold: the economic context, true enough, but not sufficient to leave millions of families stranded for decades, and the legal shortcomings of the report, as the text itself contains several types of family leave whose legal foundations are incompatible.
Consider parental leave, which appears in the text alongside maternity and paternity leave.
Personally, as an adoptive mother and on behalf of all those I represent, I appreciate the intention to grant rights equivalent to those of biological mothers.
I am in fact the additional small package referred to by Mrs. Morin, to be added to the Estrela report.

Mr. President, if the objective is truly and sincerely to strengthen the health and rights of women in the labor market—of all women—then adoptive mothers, who have become mothers in the same way as others, are entitled to the same rights and the same protection at work.
They are full-fledged mothers, just like any others; indeed, whether they adopt a child under or over twelve months old makes no difference—please, no discrimination, as stated in the text.
Regarding adoption, therefore, I regret that the text has not been explored in greater depth.
It was not even informed by the Ramboll impact study.
None of this is particularly well-developed, which is clearly a weakness.

Nevertheless, despite these reservations, I will support Mrs. Estrela's report, as beyond economic considerations, there are men and women who must not only better assume their parental responsibilities in a society of

He is increasingly disengaged from the education of his children, yet moreover, it is our duty to ensure that no one has to choose between sacrificing their children for their work or sacrificing their work for their children.
Finally, we do not sit on the Council, but in Parliament. If we, directly elected representatives, are not ambitious, then tell me who will be.
Madam President, Commissioner, President of the Council, I would like to intervene without revisiting the debate as it has unfolded thus far.

We discussed the 2011 budget under the constraints we are aware of, namely those arising from the deep crisis currently affecting Europe, with public deficits at the national level, which may lead to two hypotheses.
Do national public deficits constrain the European deficit, or must these national deficits provide a new direction and renewed momentum at the European level?
That is where the question lies.
I ask you, Mr. Commissioner, above all to try to consider, when discussing a budgetary revision, when we begin to talk about budgetary perspectives beyond 2013, not to
It cannot remain entirely shaped by the context of the crisis, unless it tells me and tells all the citizens of Europe that, definitively, Europe will remain in crisis for a long time.
Our main challenge, indeed, is managing the crisis and reducing our deficits and debt, but it is also about preparing for exit from the crisis.
It is there that the budgetary question arises.

We cannot overcome the crisis—this was immediately stated by Joseph Daul—unless the European budget, because it is European, is able to provide us with the means to regain what we lack today in terms of growth, job creation, innovation, and research.

That is the issue at stake, and it is against this standard that we must discuss the budget—not continually, as we too often do, introducing the questions of crisis and deficits. And here, the Council is excellent.

I noted that in Douvilles, Angela Merkel and Nicolas Sarkozy even announced in advance a reform of the treaties without having discussed anything whatsoever with Mr. Van Rompuy, in order to sanction all states that fail to respect budgetary discipline tomorrow.

This is where we stand today with regard to the Council, while the Parliament simply asks one thing—particularly of the Commission—to decide to advance the European project, and this should be done through the budget.
You have noted a number of points on which we completely agree.
More flexibility is needed.
Of course, we must jointly find a way to establish a much more flexible budget, as this is a matter of necessity and concerns Europe's ability to respond effectively.
It also involves, of course, setting priorities and organizing the efficiency of the European budget.
We will all agree on this, absolutely, it is a necessity.
But where we must be clear with each other is that we need a schedule, we need to set objectives together, Commissioner. It cannot remain merely words, mere commitments, mere

These are texts that are furthermore very interesting, which I share almost entirely, but now we must move into the concrete, and in particular address a crucial issue around which we keep circling: own resources.
How can we secure our own resources for tomorrow for Europe—not for Europe alone, but for all European citizens—and together regain the path to growth and forge an even stronger future for Europe?
It is your turn, Mr. Commissioner.
Mr. President, I would like to focus my remarks on the preparation for the upcoming G20 summits.

Everything has already been said by previous speakers regarding European economic governance, on the need to strengthen European economic governance, to reinforce the Stability and Growth Pact, and to involve the European Parliament and, of course, national parliaments more effectively in the process.
I would like to briefly mention two challenges facing our nations—and the members of the G20—that, in my view, must be addressed within the framework of the upcoming G20 meetings.
the challenge of currency wars and that of commodity price volatility.
I believe that on these two issues, which constitute genuine threats to global growth and cause very significant imbalances on our planet, the Union must

The European Commission is managing to organize itself, to establish common positions, and to equip itself to speak with one voice at the upcoming G20 summits—Seoul and those that follow—and more broadly on the global stage.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, I would first like to congratulate our colleague Diogo Feio on the quality of the report and on the ambitious proposal it contains.
Thus, this also demonstrates that the European Parliament is fully asserting its role in a debate that is decisive for Europe's future, and we can only congratulate ourselves on this.
Moreover, the Greek crisis served as a revelation of the dysfunctions undermining the economic governance of the European Union.
I note Mr. Feio's proposal to establish a permanent mechanism for financial stability.
We must also address the problem at its root.
To this end, I believe the solution also lies in strengthening the Stability and Growth Pact, and in particular its sanctions.
This is essential if we are to achieve a sustainable improvement in the fiscal situation of the states, although this is not always popular.
It is burdensome, but we have no choice.

Mr. President, I believe the report clearly highlights the challenges posed by the marine environment in terms of fishing, aquaculture, transportation, tourism, energy, mineral and biological resources, and research, all combined with climate change.
It is a considerable development potential that must be secured, protected, and sustainably enhanced, as the future of humanity depends on it.
The report also rightly notes that the European Union is the world's leading maritime power and that its overseas regions provide it with the largest exclusive economic zone in the world.
Given such a situation, it is therefore up to the European Union to implement an ambitious policy, a major maritime policy.
If not the world's leading maritime power, who else will do it in its place?
But it must be acknowledged that the resolution does not go very far in this direction.
One cannot claim a leadership position in global politics with just 50 million euros.
Finally, although the outermost regions have a recognized strategic position, as they are present in all oceans, it would have been possible to reduce their number.
It was prudent to assign them a major role in a comprehensive ocean strategy, which regrettably was not retained in the report, Mr. President, and I regret this.
Madam President, Commissioner, I would like to return to the anti-counterfeiting agreement currently being negotiated, particularly with the United States.
This agreement also raises questions in the United States, particularly among officials at the United States Patent Office.
Moreover, 75 American law professors have just sent a letter to President Obama.
They consider that ACTA, as a mere executive agreement, would be contrary to the United States Constitution.
While, as you know, the European Parliament is preparing to adopt a resolution on the ACTA agreement, this agreement continues to raise significant concerns and remains far from unanimous.
Among our fellow citizens, I believe it would be useful to receive from our American counterparts a very clear response on two points: Can ACTA be ratified by the United States?
Is ACTA, in their view, a binding or a voluntary agreement?
Indeed, an anti-counterfeiting agreement from which China, India, and Brazil are already absent, and which would not be implemented by the United States, would lose much of its credibility.
Thank you.

Mr. President, throughout this difficult process of adopting the alternative investment funds directive, I have consistently advocated for the principle of equal treatment between fund managers, regardless of whether they are based in the European Union or not.
This principle sounds like an obvious truth, but this obviousness has encountered countless difficulties.
The result presented to us today is satisfactory in nature.
I thank the negotiators, and above all our rapporteur, Mr. Gozes, who, following this particularly painful experience for him, deserves a substantial maternity leave.
I would particularly like to emphasize that the issue of treating third countries must still be viewed from two perspectives, whether one is inside or outside the European Union.
Thanks in particular to the European Parliament, all funds marketed within the European Union will effectively be subject to comparable regulation.
One can therefore speak, to a greater or lesser extent, of a level playing field.

It remains that, during the transitional period, non-European entities will benefit from the national placement regime, while EU funds and managers will be subject to the Directive, which is generally stricter.
The situation is different on markets located outside the European Union, where European funds and their managers will remain subject to the directive, unlike funds from third countries.
It would therefore be much more difficult to sell European products around the rest of the world, as they would be less flexible and more expensive.
We must be aware of this disadvantage, even though, to some extent, it was unavoidable.

As for the custodians, please note that we will also make the exercise more demanding, particularly due to the new responsibilities that will burden them, which should lead to substantial changes in their profession.

Mr. President, I am not saying this to demonstrate my opposition, but to emphasize that this new regulation is by no means trivial, and that its implementation, while necessary, will also entail difficulties of which we must be aware.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, particularly at the beginning of the 21st century, patients need appropriate information in order to be fully involved in their care pathway, to know where to seek care, to decide on the most suitable treatment in agreement with healthcare professionals, and to strictly follow the prescribed treatment.
Today, patients demand independent, comparative, and personalized information tailored to their individual needs.
Unfortunately, the revision proposals for the directive submitted by the European Commission did not adequately address the needs expressed by European citizens.
These proposals opened the door to direct promotion by pharmaceutical companies, which appears to us as nonsensical in terms of addressing patients' actual needs.
This operation holds no interest for either citizens or member states.
On the contrary, it meant additional bureaucracy, increased expenses, and putting patients at risk.
For these reasons, we had initially proposed rejecting these texts, thereby requesting the Commission to revise its draft.

This approach, also adopted by my colleague Carl Schilter from the Green Group, has enabled our rapporteur, Mr. Feischner, whom I salute, to carry out a significant revision of the Commission's proposals.

Meanwhile, we had tabled around sixty amendments aimed at further protecting the health of our citizens and facilitating patients’ access to independent, comparative information tailored to individual needs.

The adoption of these amendments in the Environment Committee enabled greater emphasis to be placed on patients' rights to information, rather than making the provision of information an option for the pharmaceutical industries.
Given the compromise amendments achieved, we have decided to withdraw our proposal to reject the texts, as the substance of the text has evolved in a positive direction over the past few months.
The draft report, as amended, has managed to counterbalance the European Commission's initial proposals.

However, we will continue to oppose the instrumentalization of healthcare professionals in distributing to their patients brochures and information provided by pharmaceutical companies, without any oversight by health authorities.
Likewise, we will oppose the authorization of industrial information campaigns, particularly with regard to vaccination.
Information cannot freely come from pharmaceutical companies.

Experience shows that we must be extremely vigilant, and indeed, to conclude, what occurred with mediator drugs demonstrates just how vigilant we must be and how essential it is to maintain that balance, as we have sought to achieve with this draft report.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, Madam Minister, the Union and realism will be demonstrated, at least I hope so, in tomorrow’s vote on our resolution concerning the sixteenth Conference of the Parties on climate change.

I therefore hope that our commissioner in charge will be able to pursue the practical objectives we collectively need to achieve, as well as defend the indispensable conditions set by Europe in this regard.
In these negotiations, we must likewise not lose sight of the legitimate expectations of fairness held by our fellow citizens and our industries.
It is the world that must combat climate change, not Europe alone.
Thus, recognizing the importance of intellectual property that is insufficiently protected will be essential.
Green technologies must benefit the greatest number, but we must not thereby undervalue our existing and future knowledge, even in this indispensable fight against climate change.

Similarly, requesting common calculation methods for emissions, as well as the verification and publication of data, so that all emitting industries are subject to the same standard, appears to be a highly legitimate demand and not a negotiating tactic.
Finally, more controversial—and my colleague raised this point earlier—gaps in the current system of clean development mechanisms will also need to be addressed.
Dear colleagues, we are all fully aware, regretfully, that the true negotiating power does not lie with the European Parliament.

But the determination of many European deputies, including myself, to work toward and contribute to a positive outcome of this conference must be encouraged, not overlooked, as we felt during the past day.
If Europe wishes to be strong within international dialogues, it must be equally strong among all these legislators.
Madam President, the European Union often presents economic partnership agreements as an opportunity for African countries.
She therefore despair of not seeing these agreements signed as soon as possible.
But the question that concerns us is why these countries do not rush to sign agreements that would be so favorable to them.
Clearly, opinions differ regarding the quality of these agreements, and as long as these differences in assessment persist, ACP countries will hesitate to sign them.
Would it not be appropriate to go further in dialogue and understanding by listening to the concerns and expectations of the ACP countries?
These ones wish to discuss development strategy, whereas Europe often responds with market opening.
It is this listening that the ACP-Europe Parliamentary Assembly imposes upon itself.
I believe other European bodies should adopt the same approach if we are to establish a genuine strategic partnership between Africa and the European Union.
Mr. President, Christians are undoubtedly one of the most persecuted religious groups in the world.

In the Middle East, they are perceived by certain extremist groups as, and I quote, legitimate targets, and the objective is their outright departure from this region—this region where Christianity was born and where their historical presence is essential to a certain balance.
The events at the cathedral in Baghdad are merely the climax of a situation we have passively observed for far too long.
As a global actor and commercial partner of numerous countries in this region, the European Union has a duty to strongly express its indignation in light of the situation.
Discrimination of any kind cannot be tolerated.
Democracy will prevail in this region only when fundamental human rights, such as religious freedom, are respected.
Dear colleagues, we are all deeply concerned by the serious crisis currently taking place in Côte d'Ivoire.

It is clear that the decision by this country's Constitutional Council to challenge, in violation of the electoral code, the results of elections officially proclaimed by the independent electoral commission has been universally condemned by the international community.

The European Union, the United Nations, the African Union, the Parity Parliamentary Assembly of the European Union-ACP, the COAS, and soon undoubtedly the European Parliament, are demanding respect for the official proclamation.

the election results and the installation of the new President of the Republic, as recognized by the Independent Electoral Commission, which, I remind you, is an institution validated by the United Nations.
All political groups in the European Parliament have expressed, in a joint statement, their agreement on these essential points.

They also called for halting any escalation of tensions within the country that could lead to civil war, which would represent a human, social, and economic disaster for Côte d'Ivoire—something that must obviously be avoided at all costs.

Madam President, I would like in turn to thank our four rapporteurs, who have carried out outstanding work and have significantly enhanced the European Parliament’s contribution to the text initially proposed by the Commission.
The citizen initiative we are about to adopt with enthusiasm within the PPE is a major advancement of the Lisbon Treaty.
Let us not be deceived: this instrument can truly transform the European political landscape, provided it is used with both responsibility and courage—I repeat, responsibility and courage.
It can help bring European citizens directly into the European political debate.

It can help foster a European public opinion, meaning men and women who are citizens of Europe, engaging in joint debate, taking collective positions, and coming together on issues that are truly European.
But let us not disappoint our fellow citizens.
We urge our member states to take swift implementation measures where necessary; let us not disappoint our fellow citizens—it is about strengthening European democracy.
Mr. President, I, too, like several of my colleagues, would like to address the issue of the persecution of Christians throughout the world, particularly Christians of the Middle East.
No one needs to be reminded of the horrible acts recently committed.
I would like to solemnly recall that defending Christians in the East, as elsewhere in the world, advocating for religious freedom, the diversity of religions...
This belief is advocating for freedom, democracy, and human rights—values at the heart of the European project, and which, of course, as we are all convinced here, are universal values.
It is truly time now, beyond outrage, emotion, and solemn condemnations—of course—truly time now to move from words to action.

I therefore urgently call upon the authorities of the European Union, the High Representative for Foreign Affairs and Security Policy, as well as all our policymakers, the ministers of foreign affairs.
Undertake precise measures, implement a clear, concrete action plan to make respecting religious diversity and combating all forms of religious persecution a priority.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, High Representative, we all agree that the situation in the Sahel region has significantly deteriorated.
Allowing a zone of lawlessness to take root at the gates of Europe is a danger we must resolutely and immediately confront.
Extremist groups are multiplying and threatening the lives of civilian populations.
On my part, I deplore the loss of human lives and the hostage takings, and I am concerned about European nationals present on the ground.
To date, Morocco had served as a barrier against all kinds of trafficking coming from the Sahel.
Now, drug, arms, and human traffickers circumvent Morocco by passing through Mauritania to the Canary Islands, en route to the European Union.
It seems to me that it is the responsibility of the European Union to act in order to assist these states in putting an end to these threats.
Europe's security is played out on our territory, as well as in its immediate neighborhood.
It is time for us to act, and I call on the Commission and the Council to implement an action plan to help restore security in the Sahel.
This has already been stated, but I join in the expressions of sympathy and support that have already been extended to the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt.
It represents a symbol of hope for all defenders of freedom.
A thought also for the victims.

I would now like to say that the European Union, which, as has been noted, has been criticized for its lack of response, must stand firmly alongside the peoples of Tunisia and Egypt to help them successfully open their countries to reform and establish democracy.

The European Union has decided to support Tunisian leaders in embarking on a peaceful transition, preparing for the upcoming elections, achieving freedom, and enabling the Tunisian people to attain prosperity through development and social peace, and an economy that will allow youth to find employment.

To conclude, I would say that the Union for the Mediterranean faces a significant challenge, as the Jasmine Revolution has triggered a wave across all the states of the Maghreb, the Middle East, and the Near East.
She must implement a robust strategy toward these countries.

But beware, in the face of this legitimate democratic movement, Europe must find a balance between non-interference in internal affairs, support for the legitimate aspirations of peoples, and stability.
Mr. Commissioner, my colleagues have reiterated this point on numerous occasions.

At a time when Europe is questioning a common strategy for its raw materials supply, and particularly for rare earths, we cannot overlook the immense potential represented by the intelligent valorization of our waste.

Strengthening European standards to improve the functioning of collection chains, address the issue of leakage or illegal export of such materials, or increase our collection targets must also be at the heart of this legislation.
Let us not forget the main actor in the success or failure of this regulation: the consumer.
Citizen accountability is crucial if we are to establish effective legislation.
To this end, my dear Carl Heinz, the eco-contribution has proven to be one of the most relevant tools, among other systems.

Electrical and electronic equipment producers must obviously take greater responsibility within the collection and recycling system, but citizens cannot be exempted from accountability for the waste they generate.
Our citizens must be actors, not spectators, of European environmental policy.
Madam President, Mr President, Commissioner, I would like to echo the observation made by Mrs McGuinness regarding the essential link between food security and agricultural policy.
The issue that concerns us is the rise in food prices, along with the potential consequences it may have on various populations around the world, as well as in Europe.
I would first like to try to share an observation.

The trend toward rising agricultural prices and agricultural commodity prices is, in my view, a long-term trend that will persist for three fundamental reasons: the demographic evolution of the world and the increasing
Second, the fact that we have returned, with climate change, to climatic hazards that pose problems—whether droughts or floods. Third, because the world is also developing.
With new emerging countries, consumption levels are increasing.
Pressure on agricultural markets and agricultural raw materials is a major trend.
It is exacerbated by price volatility, and price volatility is a consequence of the financialization of the global economy.
It is on this very issue that we must also focus if we are to resolve the agricultural and food issues.

To achieve this, what the commissioner says is important: there are two key elements—yes, stock transparency is necessary, but there also needs to be a public stock policy in Europe and global stock management, particularly at the level of the United Nations.

Second point: financial markets and financial speculation must be regulated, particularly by ensuring that speculative volumes no longer have any connection whatsoever with the physical quantities traded on these markets.

We cannot accept that, on the issue of food, speculators come to speculate without regard for the consequences this may have, since we are dealing with the food of the world and humanity. I conclude by
Because this leads me to two major consequences: we need a strong European policy, and we reaffirm strategically Europe's commitment to being a global player in food and agriculture.

Second, we need to revise our development policy so that, on a global scale—Europe being an actor in this process—we once again invest in agriculture, because we will need all forms of agriculture to feed the world.
Madam President, Commissioner, as in 2008, agricultural commodity prices are soaring on global markets.
We are only at 3 compared to the 2008 level.
The decline in production in certain traditional exporting regions due to climate change, the diversion of cereals toward biofuel production, and speculation
Financial institutions that inadvertently caused upward effects are responsible for this situation, as shown by reports from the IMF, French authorities, and also stated by the Commission.
The consequences of this new food crisis are severely affecting the 1.2 billion people worldwide who live below one dollar a day.
Since October, an additional 44 million people have joined those living below the poverty line.
Two days before the G20 meeting, what is Europe waiting for to take similar measures to those adopted in the United States?
Since January 13, 2011, the U.S. administration has required the limitation and transparency of positions taken by financial funds in agricultural commodity markets.
This advances the first step toward countering the appetites of speculators.
The impact is also dramatic for European livestock farmers.
They can no longer feed their animals.
Hundreds of farms are bankrupt.
Five years ago, wheat was worth 100 euros per tonne; today it is trading at nearly 300 euros.
The situation has therefore completely changed.
The Commission demands that farmers adapt to market signals; it would do well today to follow its own advice.
Faced with this crisis, the European Union cannot wait until 2014 to find solutions.
We must confront the crisis, and I propose three concrete actions.
First, the creation of grain stocks in cereal-producing countries to make feed available to livestock farmers at subsidized prices immediately.
Second, indexing meat prices paid to farmers to the evolution of livestock feed prices.
Thirdly, for 2011, a mechanism to transfer part of the subsidies intended for cereal support to livestock production. Thank you.
Mr. President, are we prepared in Europe to face a pandemic?
The answer is yes, but there is a caveat.
The 2009 flu outbreak in Chania showed us that each Member State reacted on its own behalf, without much consistency, nor even solidarity.

As the fictitious rapporteur on this file, I believe it was necessary to make these observations, but above all to go further, to be constructive and concrete for our citizens, who, we must acknowledge—and this is, in my view, the most regrettable aspect—have likely lost some confidence in public health messages.

Ms. Rivasi's report, which will be voted on tomorrow, takes into account these fundamental priorities, which include, among others, the systematic strengthening of cooperation among Member States, and improved coordination with the
European health institutions, clarification of the roles of European health risk management structures, and an assessment of vaccination and communication strategies implemented by member states.
It is therefore a good report; I congratulate the rapporteur. Naturally, it must not remain a dead letter.
It is the responsibility of our member states, which retain the majority of competences in this area, and thus represents a genuine responsibility to be upheld toward our citizens.
Mr. President, I too wish to express my profound emotion following the cowardly assassination on March 2nd of Mr. Bati, the Pakistani Minister responsible for Christian minority affairs.
This terrorist murder follows numerous other assassinations of courageous, tolerant Pakistanis who love human rights, human rights defenders, and defenders of women's rights, I must say.

Once again, we urgently call on the Pakistani government to shed full light on this cowardly assassination and to ensure effective, truly effective protection for those threatened by fanatical religious extremists.
I think, of course, above all, but not exclusively, of Mrs. Sherry Rehmann.
I also wished, once again, forcefully to call upon all competent institutions of the European Union to include in the negotiations aimed at

I would like to prepare the future cooperation agreements, in particular the cooperation agreement between the Union and Pakistan, including a clause on respect for religious freedom and human rights.
Mr. President, for the record, due to the crisis and the bursting of the real estate and credit bubbles, the Irish State was forced to intervene to support the banks.
Ireland's public deficit, the cumulative budget balance of central, local, and particularly social security administrations, was estimated at 32 percent of gross domestic product in 2010.

If the Irish government, which finds it difficult to refinance itself on financial markets, wishes to obtain loans from the mechanism and the European Financial Stability Facility, it will have to comply with obligations concerning social and fiscal reforms, even though these matters fall under the principle of subsidiarity.
In our view, this is not contradictory.
As a physician, I would say that in the case of fever, it is pointless to prescribe antipyretics blindly without seeking to treat the underlying cause.
In the event of the allocation of these funds, it is up to that State to carry out a financial correction in order to eliminate its excessive deficit and rectify its previous errors.
This austerity plan should increase GDP by 10 percent over the next four years.

Just as a private individual, a bank will not intervene in their private life or management but will condition a personal loan on a guarantee of solvency, so too can the European Union lend to Ireland on the basis of consolidated solvency.
Without interfering in its private management, the bank will encourage the individual to negotiate, for example, a salary increase or to borrow elsewhere.

Thus, respecting subsidiarity, the European Union, based on a memorandum established with the IMF, considers that this solvency requires reducing the statutory minimum wage and adjusting pensions, without exempting itself from fiscal measures.
It is up to Ireland to decide whether or not to implement these measures.

It is not a free pass for European Union interference in areas of subsidiarity that would, for example, dangerously impose a European minimum wage level, but rather a protection of the stability of the eurozone through a process of mutual guarantee.

Of course, we should assist Ireland as we do other countries facing difficulties, as this also protects us; but not at any price, particularly not at the cost of allowing a state unable to repay its debts in the long term, thereby undermining both its own citizens and those of the entire European Union.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, the eruption of the Icelandic volcano last April, as many of us have just said, provided a painful illustration of the fragmentation and inefficiency of European airspace.
And each of us here in this chamber has been directly affected.
Five million of our fellow citizens found themselves stranded without a viable alternative means of transportation.
One year after this exceptional event, new European measures are still awaited.

A first crisis exercise, as you mentioned, Mr. Commissioner, relating to volcanic ash is scheduled for the upcoming 13th and 14th of April, under the coordination of Eurocontrol, ICAO, and IATA; however, it is essential that the AESA assume real leadership in coordinating safety measures at the European level.
It is certain that the information provided by the London and Toulouse VACs must be refined using more modern technological means.
These data, as seen last April, can however be contradictory and may lead each Member State to make decisions regarding the closure of its airspace, less motivated by information than by emotion.
In this context, the role of the AESA is essential in providing a harmonized interpretation of these data, as well as ensuring the centralization and effective dissemination of information.
Beyond informing the VACs, the AESA must establish a data collection mechanism by closely involving the Member States, airlines, and their pilots.
This centralization of information and coordination of all involved parties will prevent the chaos and inconsistencies we witnessed last year.
I therefore expect the Commission, in collaboration with the ESA, to swiftly present concrete proposals fully integrated into the implementation of the Single European Sky.
Madam President, dear colleagues, this text is important.
This is not a revolution; one cannot claim that everything was bad before and everything is perfect now.
Nevertheless, it presents a number of significant advancements at this provisional stage of our work.
In my view, the main issues concern improved regulation of distance selling, and in particular online sales, which is addressed in Chapter 3, as consumer rights in this area need to be strengthened.
E-commerce is highly beneficial; it broadens consumer choice, enables consumers to obtain the lowest prices, and also allows small and medium-sized enterprises and craftsmen to access new markets.

To make progress in this area, it was necessary to proceed in a truly European manner, that is, by focusing on a few specific points and establishing a comprehensive harmonization of everyone's rights and obligations.
Very concretely, it was essential that an Italian customer purchasing on a Belgian or German website should know that, from now on, they would enjoy the same rights and the same protection.
This is precisely what this directive will make possible.
This means that its withdrawal period will be extended to fourteen days throughout the Union.
This means he will immediately know the total amount he has to pay, without fear of an unpleasant surprise afterward.
This means that, thanks to the double click, it will properly verify its command.
Of course, all of this will disrupt certain habits.
In many states, there are subtleties in national law that everyone is convinced are fundamental, even though sometimes not a single trace of them can be found in neighboring countries.
But living together in Europe means learning to slightly modify these habits in the service of the common and general interest.
Let us be clear: this text will still need to be improved, and we will have to do so in the coming weeks, in collaboration with the Council and the Commission.
I appreciate the openings that have just been made in this regard.
But we have already carried out substantial work thanks to our rapporteur, Andreas Schwab, whom I warmly thank.
But the real outcome of this work is that the amendments will be voted on tomorrow by all political groups.
Consumer confidence in the EU's internal market is very fragile.
This political consensus was necessary to preserve it and to hope to strengthen it.
At paragraph 7, after the first subparagraph, a first indent ensures respect for human rights and fundamental freedoms, in particular the freedom of the person.
The freedom of speech and assembly, and the right to peacefully demonstrate, to listen to and address the legitimate demands of demonstrators, and to ensure their safety.
Madam President, dear colleagues, Commissioner, the Open Skies agreement between Europe and the United States is a step forward, but it is only that—a step that places Europe at a crossroads.
The Transport Committee and the Commission itself have highlighted this point.
Either we accept an agreement that disadvantages European companies, or we suspend the agreement and risk losing the benefits gained since 2008.
Let me therefore draw a parallel with private and professional pilot licenses, a text that will be discussed before summer, as pilots—including myself—find themselves facing a dilemma.
The European Union Aviation Safety Agency, seeking to harmonize licenses across the European territory, will do so at the expense of 90 general aviation pilots.
directly affected, some holding only a single U.S. license and at the expense of, and this is crucial, businesses and jobs related to this activity.
