Mr. President, I wish to draw your attention to this project, which lacks any security basis.
There are 31 times fewer accidents among holders of an American license.
The objectives of basic regulation text 216 allow for the recognition and equivalence of licences, making it difficult to see the benefit of waiting for hypothetical agreements postponed until 2014.
Thus, I propose an open sky agreement and even invite you to accompany me when I occasionally come to Parliament, personally piloting to demonstrate that an American pilot's license is entirely safe.
Madam President, at this stage of our debate, I am inevitably going to repeat or emphasize certain points that have already been made.

But I also wanted to say that, like many of my colleagues, the challenges we are facing—challenges that the entire European Union is currently confronting—cannot be resolved by any single member state alone.
No Member State should be left alone facing the challenges it encounters, which, once again, in my view, confront the entire European Union.
I am therefore calling precisely for the European Union's capacity to organize itself and to respond collectively, in a solidarity-based manner.
I believe the time has truly come now to lay the foundations for a genuine European immigration and asylum policy.
Over a year ago, a European pact on asylum and immigration had been adopted.
The time has now come, I believe, to present it in a very concrete manner.
Of course, Madam Commissioner, we have heard a certain number of recommendations you made.
I wanted to return to this topic and emphasize very strongly certain of these proposals.
Yes, Frontex's resources must be strengthened at all costs.
Therefore, the human, material, and financial resources of the agency Frontex must be strengthened, enabling it to provide more robust support to the Hermes and Poseidon operations.

Yes, additional financial resources are required—resources specifically for member states that, I would say, are at the forefront, the first line of response, to the intensified migration flows we are experiencing today.
This will be the financial expression of our solidarity.

Yes, closer cooperative relations must be established with the countries on the southern shore of the Mediterranean, to support them, of course, to assist them, and to identify appropriate instruments.
Madame Commissioner, I believe you mentioned that you went to Tunisia to discuss mobility partnerships.
Yes, these instruments must be strengthened in a spirit of responsibility, in a spirit of solidarity, but also in a precise and concrete spirit.

Mr. President, Commissioner, you are undoubtedly aware that the most intense discussions we have had here regarding your communication concern the role of public services, services of general interest, within the single market, and their legislative implementation.
We have reached a compromise formulation that invites you to go beyond your Proposal 25, and we ask you to go beyond mere

communication that would fall short of the expectations and needs of the actors, users, and all stakeholders of public services, and that would likewise fail to meet the objectives you yourselves have set.
The European Union must convey a clear and unambiguous message regarding public services.
To achieve this, we must establish a positive legal framework, such as a regulation or directive.
The Treaty of Lisbon provides us with the legal foundations.
It is up to you to translate them now into legislative proposals.
I believe the Parliament is ready to follow you down this path.
Mr. President, first of all, congratulations to all the rapporteurs for their immense work in connection with this discharge, and I also welcome the presence of the Council for this debate.
The seat of the European Parliament in Strasbourg is a matter of European identity, and it is not merely an economic issue.

I am addressing this issue today in this Chamber during the 2009 discharge debate, because the symbol of Franco-German reconciliation is being treated by some members of the Committee on Budgetary Control as a source of revenue, a mere means of saving money.
The issue of Parliament's expenditures also interests me.
On this matter, I propose holding the group sessions in Strasbourg rather than Brussels, which would allow us to make better use of our Parliament's seat in Strasbourg.
Since we wish to achieve savings, I propose other measures, for example, eliminating the Friday half-day off for our colleagues, as well as doing away with half-day offs for journalists.
Europe is sufficiently attractive for us not to have to grant indulgences to journalists.

Let us not hide behind the budgetary argument in order to appropriate an important issue that goes beyond the scope of budgetary oversight, even though I commend Ville, our rapporteur, for his important work on this discharge.
Finally, since we are discussing economies, I also wish for the system of indemnification for sponsored visitor groups to be moralized and cleansed.

I find this very unethical, and it gives our institution a very poor image, for the group leader to be reimbursed in cash on the day of the visit, without having performed any work or provided any oversight of the group's expenditures.
Sometimes, the amount allocated—please note this—exceeds the actual expenses incurred.

It would be logical for groups to submit a quotation in advance to the Visits Service, receive a parliamentary account of expenditures and have this managed through a bank account, with the balance paid upon presentation of actual invoices, of course subject to a clear ceiling. Thank you.
Madam President, I am addressing the Commission, my colleagues, and I welcome the presence of the Council.
The question arising today stems from the management of the Schengen area in 2011.

This is no longer the space of the five Member States of the 1980s; on the contrary, it has continuously expanded in recent years and now comprises twenty-five states, three of which are not members of the European Union.
Like all European policies, the Schengen cooperation must therefore adapt to the reality it currently encompasses, which is no longer the same as it was ten years ago.

It is in this sense that one must interpret the proposals put forward by the Italian and French governments and the pragmatic and necessary responses provided today by the Commission in its communication on immigration.
Mutual trust among the States parties to the Schengen area is indeed the cornerstone of Schengen cooperation.
But this trust is based on mutual obligations.
If one of the parties fails to meet its obligations and creates air leaks, the entire system becomes impaired.
It must therefore be possible for other states to address this failure by reestablishing temporary and strictly limited control over these borders.
In doing so, these failing states will be clearly encouraged to better control their external borders.

Moreover, as presented by the Commission, these measures will fully respect the spirit of Schengen, inasmuch as such controls would be reintroduced only as a last resort and the decision would be made at the European level, not on an intergovernmental basis.
Such measures will be inherently linked to the broader range of measures envisaged in the long term, within the framework of a common migration policy.
It is particularly essential to strengthen Frontex, to provide greater solidarity, I conclude, in support of the Member States located at the external borders.
The situation experienced by the Mediterranean in recent years merely highlights a gap in the Schengen acquis.
Madam President, within a few weeks, migrants coming from countries bordering the Mediterranean have accounted for a significant proportion of annual French immigration.
This achievement is not static, but must instead evolve.
I hope these proposals will be adopted swiftly, in order to ensure as soon as possible improved management of our Schengen area and, thereby, the security of our common external borders. Thank you.
Mr. President, the European Union has finally imposed sanctions on Syria.
But unfortunately, she appears to do so with a trembling hand, carefully avoiding any challenge to the head of the Syrian regime.
This lack of determination and clarity is obviously interpreted by demonstrators as a form of abandonment, and by the authorities as indulgence that enables them to intensify the struggle.
Such a cruel crackdown that one cannot even imagine for a moment that, at the same time, Syria could be elected to sit on the United Nations Human Rights Council.
of the United Nations, while today itself the Syrian authorities deny United Nations observers access to the city of Deraa, the site of terrible violence committed against unarmed civilians.

An assassination at the Human Rights Council would be an affront to victims and their families, an encouragement for this regime to resort to brutal force against its people, in flagrant disregard of the very values that the Human Rights Council is meant to uphold.
Madam High Representative, what diplomatic options are you considering to prevent such a disaster?

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would first like to congratulate my colleague Carlos Coelho, who has produced masterful work on this report, and indeed, as everyone knows, it was extremely complex.
Bulgaria and Romania have made considerable efforts to strengthen control over their borders, and they have succeeded.
Whether it concerns police and judicial cooperation, data protection, visa policy, or the control of air, maritime, or land borders, the latest assessments are very clear.
All technical criteria are met.
We can only acknowledge this factual situation.
Bulgaria and Romania are now in a position to implement Schengen acquis both technically and in terms of staffing.
Moreover, although no obstacles currently prevent the expansion of the Schengen area, we note that a number of recommendations have been made to these two countries.

I hope these recommendations will be strictly followed, particularly with regard to the borders between Bulgaria, Greece, and Turkey, as you know, mutual trust among Schengen States rests on these States fully respecting their reciprocal obligations.
If one of the parties fails to meet its obligations, it creates air gaps; the entire system then begins to fail.
I very much hope that an agreement will be reached at the Council to admit these two countries into the privileged circles formed by the Schengen States.
And we shall all, in this hemicycle, assess the significance of this kind of decision for the affected citizens.
Well done once again to Mr. Coelho for his report, Mr. President.
Mr. President, in its Europe 2020 strategy, the European Union has set itself the goal of making our economy greener and our transport systems more modern.
All European institutions have strongly supported this objective, including the vast majority of member states and our Parliament.
In this context, it is therefore regrettable that, since 2008, several Member States have sought to undermine the substance of the new Eurovignette directive proposal and have blocked this file.

Today, however, thanks to the perseverance of our rapporteur—whom I would like to salute here, Saeed El Khadraoui—and thanks to his determination as well as that of the shadow rapporteurs in their negotiations with the Council, we finally have a compromise before us.

Thus, certainly, this text is considerably less ambitious than the initial proposal, but it will for the first time allow the implementation of the polluter-pays principle and the internalization of external costs of transport, such as noise or CO2 emissions.

For this eurovignette to be more than just a tax and genuinely contribute to improving transport across Europe, Member States are likewise encouraged to reinvest the collected funds in modernizing transport modes.
The Parliament has also managed to achieve concrete progress regarding the interoperability of toll systems, as well as stronger incentives for the use of less polluting heavy goods vehicles.

In order to remain consistent with the political objectives set by our institution, I sincerely hope that we shall be able to adopt the compromise proposed at second reading and thus pave the way for a more sustainable transport policy.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I agree that, alongside freedom and fraternity, there must be equality with farmers and countries that have returned, with countries returning from the East.
Yes, I agree with you.
I believe we have made a significant advance with this report.
It wasn't easy at first, and we were rather in a state of confrontation, but we managed to reach a consensus, and that is what matters most.
This consensus helps safeguard the overall project, and I believe that is the great merit of the European Parliament.
We have achieved this goal, and it was no easy task; I therefore thank the rapporteur Mercedes and all the parliamentarians.
First step.
In this text, we reaffirm the necessity of equality, justice, and fairness both among producers and among territories, and I believe this is important.
We have stated very clearly, and confirmed, the maintenance and development of small farms, taking into account small farms, which are essential for the quality and biodiversity of territories.
I believe it was equally fundamental.
The fact that the concept of making agriculture agronomic, of turning the page on productivist industrial agriculture, has been incorporated into the texts.
We must now put this into practice; it is a prerequisite for making agriculture possible for future generations.
This is not an ideological issue.
Finally, today, our concern is that there are indeed threats to the budget.
It will be necessary to respond to it clearly.

Now, what we are awaiting is that we actually enter the second phase, which is clearly the legislative debate, because beyond texts and broad intentions, it is in the legislative debate that we will truly see where we are headed, and that is precisely what we naturally expect now.
I would still like to conclude by expressing outrage at Germany's stance, which led to a reduction of five in food aid to the poorest in Europe.
It was within the CAP budget.
I want us to find a way to help the poorest; there are 80 million European citizens who do not have enough proper food to eat.

Mr. President, while the European Parliament can rightly claim several significant achievements in economic governance, I urge my colleagues to exercise humility, for we are dancing on the edge of a precipice.
Let us be fully aware of two things.
The first is that the European Parliament remained unheard during the Greek crisis.
While, ordinarily, we are so quick to respond to all kinds of events with resolutions everywhere around the world, this existential crisis seems to leave us without a path.
I deeply regret that we have been unable to send a strong, concrete, and clear message to the Greeks, to the governments of the member states, and to European citizens.
The second observation relates to the scope of this package.
By voting for what I wish—these six directives or regulations—we will indeed take a significant step forward for the eurozone.
Not a single step toward a discipline that has been so lacking for us, I say, nothing more than a step that will demonstrate its usefulness in the future.
But one need only observe the heated debates surrounding the overturned qualified majority to gauge how far we still have to go, when it should be rules and automatic sanctions that are at issue.
The mechanism of reversed qualified majority continues to act as a scarecrow to some.
We must nevertheless remain firm on this principle.
From a strictly political standpoint, I regret that the benches of the left continue to reject the principles of sound budgetary management, despite all evidence to the contrary.
Yes, deficit and sustainable growth are contradictory.
Yes, states must also manage their budgets in a prudent manner, but not through public spending stimulus. It is a mild poison in the short term, but it is never a sustainable solution.
As the coming weeks prove pivotal, it is now essential to rebuild an economic union.
This morning, after three years of work, we voted on a very important text for advancing consumer protection in Europe.
He rightly focuses on internet purchases, which may, in twenty years, account for 25 percent of all commerce in Europe—a figure that is entirely considerable.
These jassas will notably be transboundary.
They will allow consumers to broaden their choices and also pay significantly less.
This is a real advancement.
For small and medium enterprises, as well as for all craftsmen, this also represents a very important opportunity to expand their market reach.

Among the main advancements, I would like to mention just two: the extension of the withdrawal period to fourteen days, and the summary of all information and the total price to be paid before pressing the final button that places the order.

And finally, I am proud, in the context of these voting explanations, to say that I, along with all my colleagues, voted unanimously across political groups, because on such a matter, what we needed was not controversy, but obviously consensus.
It was us who obtained it.

Madam President, Madam and Mr. Commissioners, I would first like to commend our colleague Carlos Coelho for his efforts on this very challenging file, which requires great expertise, considerable work, and in-depth knowledge of the subject.
The control of our shared territory and European borders is one of the most important challenges we face, and technological tools are more essential than ever in meeting this challenge.
Indeed, over the past few years, we have witnessed an unprecedented development of European databases and the exchange of personal data.
What some refer to as a digital tsunami is certainly expected to materialize in the coming years.
The SIS, the VIS, and EURODAC are all essential tools today for ensuring the security of our European citizens.
Ensuring the effective operational management of these databases is therefore a major issue, one to which it is our responsibility to provide an efficient response.

This response is, of course, highly delicate, and Carlos Cuello had to overcome numerous legal, institutional, budgetary, political, and technical obstacles, yet he ultimately produced a balanced text that satisfies all interested parties.
The establishment of this network agency appears to be the best solution for ensuring the internal security of the Member States.
No other existing institution or agency could currently assume responsibility for the proper management of these information systems.

It will enable improved management of existing information systems, provide better synergy among these databases, and furthermore allow for the integration of tools that will be adopted in the future.
One of the most delicate issues raised in this matter was the location of the agency—where should it be established?
Estonia and France are the only two countries that have put forward their candidacy to host it.
France already hosts in Strasbourg the Schengen Information System, which it has managed operationally and administratively since 1995, thereby possessing full technical mastery over it.
Following the conclusions of the December 2003 European Council, which prioritized the new Member States, Estonia also wished to host the seat of this future agency.

Mr. President, it seems to me that the compromise reached is very good. A kind of division of responsibilities has been established, Madam President, by relocating the agency's headquarters to Tallinn, where governance, strategy, and program design tasks will be carried out.
The development and operation of the systems will take place in Strasbourg.

The cost-effectiveness ratio is very well balanced, and this compromise solution in no way undermines the centralized management of IT systems. Madam President, I am very pleased to have spoken to you about this report.
Mr. President, the horizontal social clause and Article 14 of the Lisbon Treaty provide binding guarantees of social rights, including the right of access to services of general interest.
It is time to show European citizens that the social Europe is moving forward.

We must therefore grant our social achievements the full place they deserve within community law, by recognizing the specific nature of SSI in relation to competition rules, bearing in mind that these services have a strong local foundation and are based on actors from the social economy.

Mr. De Rossa's report provides an opportunity to reaffirm the simple yet powerful and meaningful principles of equality, universality, and access to these collectively organized services, which are key factors in well-being and social progress.
Madam Commissioner, you said it was time to get down to concrete actions.
Yes, it is time to move to concrete action, so I propose, in this debate, that we genuinely support a European legislative instrument for social public services.
I therefore invite you to vote in favor of the alternative resolution submitted by the Greens group.
Mr. President, debates concerning GMOs must necessarily be based on irrefutable arguments, both scientifically and legally.
From a legal standpoint, Member States wishing to restrict or ban GMOs on their territory required arguments that were both diverse and robust.
The rapporteur has broadened the range of available options, but remains nonetheless vague.
- I don't know.
This foundational scientific document must likewise be irrefutable.
The Council has repeatedly challenged the scientific assessments of the EFSA, which otherwise consistently favor GMOs, in contrast to national scientific evaluations.

To restore trust, Mr. Commissioner, would it not be advisable, first and foremost, simply to enforce the existing legislation, which provides considerable scientific guarantees, particularly regarding long-term impact assessment?
Why have the recommendations of the Council from October 2008 remained unimplemented?
Madam President, Madam Commissioner, first of all, I would like to thank our colleague Simon Busuttil for the outstanding work he has accomplished.
These reflections have enriched the initial proposal, and we can only congratulate it for that.
This report outlines an agency with resources multiplied tenfold, better aligned with the missions assigned to it.
Recent events have shown us the need for closer cooperation among the European Union, its member states, and the support of a robust agency.
Frontex finally has the means to fulfill its missions.
By being able to acquire or rent its own equipment and by benefiting from its own border guards, it becomes more independent and highly effective.
New tasks are also assigned to him, such as organizing voluntary returns and processing personal data, so that we may combat cross-border crime while safeguarding fundamental rights.

This extension of Frontex's mandate provides us with the means to address migration issues through a comprehensive approach, rather than confining ourselves to a mere mechanical management of borders.
I approve the possibility of exchanging personal data with other European agencies, particularly Europol.
Finally, the human dimension has taken its full place in this report.
A strong commitment to human rights is ensured by the proposal to establish an advisory council within the Agency.

I can only welcome this comprehensive proposal, which represents a step forward toward the sound and responsible management of our external borders, in a context marked by particularly intense and ongoing migratory pressures; thank you.

Mr. President, I would like to thank the rapporteur, Mr. Meyer, and the remarks made by my colleagues, sometimes critical. Nevertheless, this 2010 activity report finally provides a splendid opportunity to discuss the Committee on Petitions, which remains too little known.
Yet it is the Commission, which has a direct, sometimes physical, link with the citizen, that can itself attend our debates.
She has undeniable legitimacy in Parliament, as well as extensive experience, thanks to her direct relationship with the citizen.
Indeed, any petitioner may appear before Parliament and defend their rights before the Petitions Committee.
One can also commend the composure of the discussions, despite the painful cases that are frequently addressed.
This Commission represents Europe as it should be—attentive to the concerns of its citizens and united in finding solutions and safeguarding them.
Unfortunately, the available means of action and influence remain limited, particularly with regard to the environmental risks posed by projects that are sometimes nearly completed.
But I also see it when it comes to defending specific cases, such as family rights or children's rights in the event of divorce.
We will furthermore carry out a mission on this topic in November.
I also regret that national authorities do not always promptly provide us with all the necessary information.
I would like to thank the European Commission for the research and verification work it carries out, even though it could sometimes go further in seeking the truth.
I must also commend the constructive work we carry out with the European Ombudsman.
I now appeal to citizens to continue to engage us through petitions as our directly elected representatives.
Our role will always be to safeguard your interests, in strict compliance with European law.
Madam President, Commissioner, I welcome this resolution on homelessness, as it is our responsibility to underscore its extreme urgency.
Let us first note that what was a priority yesterday remains even more prejudicial today.

The lack of a clear definition and reliable statistical indicators regarding homelessness cannot prevent us from observing the worsening crisis, as the number of people living below the poverty threshold rose—from 79 million in 2009 to 84 million, according to Eurostat, an estimated figure for 2011.
Dear colleagues, three years ago, our Parliament, concerned with eradicating poverty, called on Member States to eliminate the problem of food insecurity by 2015.
Today, we ourselves are somewhat closer to our goal.
Was the year 2010, dedicated to the fight against poverty, genuinely utilized by Member States to address concretely this profound precariousness, the urgency of which has become permanent?
I genuinely doubt it and deeply regret it.

To conclude, I wish to emphasize the need to develop a comprehensive, multisectoral strategy against homelessness, as certainly there is poverty and a lack of housing, but there are also family issues, health concerns, various forms of addiction, and personal vulnerabilities.
A comprehensive, effective, ambitious yet tailored approach is required, one that takes into account individuals, the heterogeneous nature of their trajectories, and their life disruptions.
It is a right, at the very least fundamental, from which many large groups of precarious workers are deprived.
Madam President, over these past years, as everyone has said, the European Union has been struck by unprecedented disasters, and the cities here would require the entire duration of the debate.
I will nonetheless mention the Azed F explosion, which we commemorated ten years after the event less than a week ago in Toulouse.
A strengthened response at the European level, as proposed in Mrs. Gardini's report, represents a genuine opportunity to demonstrate solidarity among our populations.

For several years now, the Union has been striving to make this pooling of our efforts operational, and it is now time to act together and no longer wait for another disaster to strike our territories before learning from it.
Today, we must make available our material, human, and financial resources to ensure that no new tragedies occur.
Madam Commissioner, a moment ago, you said that risks must be anticipated.

And it is truly regrettable that, in this context, the European Commission is considering withdrawing from funding the GMES satellite observation programme, which is recognized by all as an essential tool for disaster prevention and coordination of response efforts.
I urgently call on the Commission to reconsider this position and to support these flagship programmes that provide Europe with an undeniable added value.
Here, I would like to thank Ms. Gardini for her very good report.
I hope we will adopt it, as this will thus pave the way for European solidarity serving the citizen. Thank you.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Minister, the number of poor and unemployed people continues to rise within the European Union.
More than 43 million Europeans are at risk of food poverty.
The hour is grave.
Today, six member states wish to terminate the food aid programme without any replacement solution: Germany, Sweden, Denmark, the United Kingdom, the Netherlands, and the Czech Republic.
Certainly, there is a ruling from the Court of Justice, and we must respect it.
However, Mr. Commissioner, Parliament addressed you last July, at the initiative of our group, the Greens, and you made relevant proposals to safeguard the programme.
The Greens are ready to work with you on long-term solutions, but given that millions of people depend on this system, we cannot allow the food aid budget to be reduced by a factor of four.
The urgency is real.
If nothing is done by the end of the year, more than 300 million euros will be missing from food banks, and there is no guarantee that national governments will be able to step in to fill the gap.
It is therefore a genuine question of solidarity among peoples.
Let us not allow a minority of irresponsible governments to deprive millions of Europeans of food aid, as this is above all a matter of survival.
Combating poverty and malnutrition also requires a reform of the CAP.
We must fundamentally change the way we produce food in order to bring food production closer to populations, particularly the poorest, who also have the right to healthy and balanced diets.
Only a truly sustainable Common Agricultural Policy will enable us to ensure that fewer Europeans will need food aid.
The Council must return to reason and allow those in need to access this vital assistance.
I am particularly addressing the future Danish government, which will be responsible for chairing the European Union starting in January.
Must there be food riots in Europe before we come to our senses and recognize the urgent need to change course?

When it comes to saving banks, the Council knows how to find solutions, but when it comes to saving food banks, suddenly no one is around. The Parliament made a strong statement last time: human beings are more important than finance.
finance, it is high time to reaffirm it.
In any case, we will remain very vigilant and will not fail to renew our call in a constructive manner.
Indeed, six member states hold in their hands the fate of 18 million Europeans in need.
Six member States that claim to be doing no more or less than legal scholarship.
Jurisprudence is one thing, humanity and political choices are another.
How can we fail to express our anger toward those who remain deaf to the alarm cries issued by organizations that assist the most vulnerable?

And we, Members of the European Parliament, representatives of all citizens, ask ourselves whether we want an Europe in which national selfishness dictates policy, paying little heed to the citizens of countries facing budgetary difficulties?
There is no question of renationalizing this aid.

No, let us maintain this program, give it a stronger legal foundation, but do not abandon those who, in the immediate present, in 2012, rely on this assistance during particularly difficult crisis periods.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, I will not dwell on the cynicism with which the ArcelorMittal group announced the closure of certain of its facilities in Europe, and in particular in Belgium, at Liège, ultimately leading to hundreds of direct layoffs and jeopardizing several thousand indirect jobs.
As a native of Liège, I share the shock and dismay of the 581 workers who have been dismissed from their family company.
But I also think of the stringent restructuring plans announced by the steel group in France, Germany, and Luxembourg.
The reasons cited are always the same: poor outlook for the development of the steel market in Europe, intense competition from imports from China and South Korea.
Russia and Brazil, the shrinking automobile market, and finally, the competitiveness gap, for example, between the Liège plant and the best factories in Northern Europe.
In addition, despite a challenging 2010 year, the group's Belgian steel company financial center generated a profit of 1.394 billion euros.
He paid zero euros in taxes, resulting in a tax rate of zero.
This is particularly shocking.
Group management proves to be primarily financial, with a stop-and-go policy implemented across multiple sites.
The closure of sites is nothing more in the minds of these leaders than an economic adjustment variable, in which human concerns are of little importance.
This is no longer acceptable.
You have spoken, Mr. Commissioner, of rules.
Would it not be time for the Commission to develop a binding code of social responsibility for multinational enterprises?

Six years already since our Parliament adopted a report on relocations, corporate conduct codes, repayment of subsidies, and mandatory consultation among companies, trade unions, and public authorities.
Since then, it must be acknowledged that little concrete progress has been made on these various proposals.
So, what does the Commission intend to do about this, and within what timeframe?
If we are to support the competitiveness of businesses and make Europe attractive in terms of economic investment, we must also adopt a legislative framework.
strong European presence to cope with offshoring and restructuring, and to ensure full recognition of another capital—the human capital.
Finally, regarding the Liège site, I hope it will be able to benefit from the Globalisation Adjustment Fund to compensate the dismissed workers. Thank you.

Madam President, dear colleagues, the wave of layoffs affecting workers across Europe should prompt us to reflect on the role we, as political leaders, must play in the face of multinational corporations acting

without any social ethics, conducting stock market-driven layoffs and relocating operations to cut costs, increase shareholder profits, and please rating agencies, disregarding social and environmental standards.
In its resolution of 20 September last, the Parliament clearly expressed its intention to support above all the workers affected by the negative consequences of restructuring.
However, we have also requested that the Commission ensure coherence between the EMFF interventions and the European Union's industrial policy, particularly through the use of structural funds.

We also insisted that the future EMF regulation include improvements so that the Fund no longer produces the arbitrary, arbitrary, and moral outcomes it currently generates for multinational corporations, as is the case with ArcelorMittal and Nokia.
Commissioner, we can no longer examine separately the issue of structural funds and the global adjustment fund without
without taking into account the corporate restructuring strategies that benefit from it, without assessing the cost-benefit ratio of a policy, I repeat, well
Mr. President, the European Ombudsman is conducting, with skill, investigations into complaints lodged against the institutions and bodies of the European Union.

This instrument is both essential for facilitating free movement—of our businesses and our citizens—and also a powerful guarantee, particularly against the critics of the Union who too often claim that the European Union is merely a bureaucratic machine without oversight.
It is clearly nonsensical—Europe is undertaking real work on itself, thanks to the European Mediators, among others.
But allow me to relay a regrettable observation, in which the Commission is under intense criticism.
First, the figures 65 and the actual figure, not 12, from investigations opened by the Ombudsman concerning the European Commission.

There are also representatives from numerous companies who come to see us to express their bewilderment regarding the Commission, and regarding a European Commission that remains deaf to their requests, particularly concerning access to documents. Likewise, with regard to the issue of transparency in decision-making.
Few people find their way around it.
And finally, from personal experience, I must admit that I have sometimes been shocked by the reactions of certain services of the European Commission present during petitions committee sessions.
As you know, the European Commission provides us with its position on petitions, a position that we always take into account and which is essential.

But it occasionally happens that representatives of the European Commission refuse to answer a Member of Parliament’s questions or hide behind jargon almost as effectively as politicians, presenting themselves to citizens while advocating their own cause.
I therefore wish to remind you that Europe must have a human face, that behind the administrative issues raised and resolved by the Ombudsman are Europeans relieved to be heard, yet disappointed by
This behavior among citizens who are negatively marked by their confrontation with institutions may foster a sense of revolt, incomprehension, and ultimately, possibly, euroscepticism.
I sincerely hope that these criticisms, as well as those of the Ombudsman against institutions in general, are heard; thank you.
Madam President, professional mobility and growth, professional mobility and the competitiveness of our European Union—these are indeed the terms in which today's issue is framed.
We, the PPE Group, have lent our support to the report by our colleague, Mrs. McLaughlin, to the preparation of which we have greatly contributed, and of course we support

This is the main recommendation of this report to enhance professional mobility, simplify and improve the efficiency of procedures for recognizing professional qualifications, while ensuring patient and consumer safety.
But it is in terms of the pathway toward growth that the issue of increasing professional mobility arises.
Moreover, the Forum for a Single Market, held in Kraków at the beginning of October, did not misjudge the situation.
who recalled that Member States should recognize the potential of the internal market to revive growth and job creation, and should act to eliminate all obstacles to completing the internal market.
The European Council last October also set as one of the essential priorities for achieving smart, sustainable growth.

This notably includes the implementation of the twelve priority proposals from the Act for the Single Market, the twelve proposals put forward by the Commission, in particular those concerning the recognition of professional qualifications.

We clearly see that it is by increasing professional mobility that we can hope to unlock the growth potential contained within our internal market, our market of 500 million people, 500 million consumers.

Professional mobility must therefore not only ensure, if we strengthen, modernize, and improve it, the free movement of our citizens within this market, but also, we hope, generate additional growth opportunities.
That is precisely why, particularly, I wish to emphasize a practical recommendation we, the PPE, have consistently supported: the implementation of a voluntary preposition policy.
Madam President, I would first like to congratulate the rapporteur, Mr. Cashmarck, on his excellent work.
I would like to emphasize that it is important each year for the European Parliament to review the work of the ACP-EU Parliamentary Assembly, inasmuch as
This assembly plays a decisive role in strengthening North-South dialogue and in establishing a development policy based on partnership and exchange.
The year 2010 was a busy one for the PPE, which met twice, as previously mentioned by my predecessors, in Spain and the DRC, to exchange views, among other matters
on the impact of climate change in ACP countries, the impact of the global crisis, or on media freedom and independence and food security.
However, in 2010, the PPE also sent its first election observation mission of this legislative term.
I had the honor of co-chairing this mission in Burundi for the presidential elections alongside my colleague Assaride, Member of Parliament from Mali.
This election observation mission was a genuine success.

We were able to meet with all the relevant political stakeholders, and thanks to the dual origin of this ACP delegation—both African and European—we embodied a legitimacy distinct from that of purely European or African missions.
I therefore invite the ACP-EU Bureau to renew this experience, which enables practical application of solidarity and the special relations binding the European Union to the ACP countries.
I would like to thank Mr. Dardenne for his very interesting report.
Certainly, the crisis is economic and financial, but above all, for our fellow citizens, it is a social crisis.
Reducing by 20 million the number of people in precarious situations by 2020 requires strong measures involving all levels of public authority.

We must more than ever fulfill the commitments made by the European Union in 2010, during the European Year for the Fight Against Poverty and Social Exclusion and at the establishment of the European Platform against Poverty and Social Exclusion, in order to have a European framework for social and territorial cohesion.

We must ensure the sustainability and optimization of European funds, particularly the current program supporting the most disadvantaged, strengthen social protection, and support local or national initiatives that develop services for the care of children, elderly individuals, and dependent persons.
Poverty is a scourge to be fought horizontally, and it is high time to act in order to enable the most vulnerable to overcome their precarious situation.

And as political leaders, at the European, national, and local levels, we must take the necessary measures to ensure, without discrimination, their access to housing, access to healthcare, access to justice, access to education, and access to a first job with a minimum income that may be adjusted according to the poverty rate in each Member State.
Investing in promoting innovation and social experimentation and in reducing poverty will enable us to establish a strategy for sustainable, smart, and inclusive growth.
The Europe we desire and currently need is a Europe based on solidarity.
Mr. President, I would first like to congratulate the rapporteur, Ms. Levin, on her excellent work.
As rapporteur for opinion of the Development Committee, I am pleased that the Fisheries Committee has taken up the elements contained in our opinion, as it is crucial that the development dimension
be taken into account in measures aimed at combating illegal fishing globally, in the interest of policy coherence and close alignment with development policy.
Indeed, combating illegal fishing requires comprehensive solutions and the involvement of all states, including developing ones.
However, it is necessary that the technical and financial capacities of States are also taken into account in the actions to be undertaken.
Indeed, one cannot impose the same constraints on developed or developing countries without accompanying measures.
Small island states, for example, have very limited capacities.
They must therefore be supported in their efforts to combat illegal fishing.
For this reason, the Union will need to increase financial support for the fisheries sector when concluding agreements in this field with developing countries.

Moreover, it is essential that measures aimed at combating illegal fishing be implemented, as this practice undermines the development of countries by reducing the financial resources these nations could otherwise derive from their fishing economy.
Otherwise, these resources are necessary for developing countries to implement social, economic, and educational policies.

Finally, it is important that the European Union promote, in implementing these measures to combat illegal fishing in developing countries, the involvement of civil society and businesses alongside the recipient states.
Adopting a holistic approach is essential to addressing the scourge of illegal fishing. Thank you.
Mr. President, I would like to say that largely thanks to these ultra-peripheral regions, the European Union is the world's leading maritime power.
This is a major advantage for Europe: the development and implementation of a comprehensive policy on seas, oceans, and seabed is essential for the future.
But I fear that with 20 or 50 million euros, it will not be necessary, nor sufficient, to achieve this major challenge.

Combatting illegal fishing is part of this broader policy, and it is urgent at the European level to find an appropriate solution to this issue, particularly within the framework of international cooperation.
I would also like to point out that the maritime zones of Guadeloupe, French Guiana, Martinique, Mayotte, and Réunion are regularly violated and plundered.
Since the report does not address this issue, I take this opportunity to draw the attention of my colleagues and the Commission to this particular matter.
Madam President, I would first like to welcome the Polish Presidency, which has demonstrated dynamism and determination.
Next week, at the Council, our heads of state and government will have to make essential decisions for the future of the eurozone and for the future of the Union.
They will also have to seriously address the revival of growth.

That said, we clearly see that today financial stability has become the paramount priority, and the pressures and tensions exerted from outside the eurozone countries, as well as those within the eurozone itself, are undermining our credibility and eroding confidence.
To hope for the return of confidence, financial stability must be restored, and the alarming rise in national deficits and sovereign debts must be broken.
Financial stability has truly become the top priority, an urgent obligation for all.

Our democracies have moreover all committed themselves to very courageous programs for restoring public finances and reducing their debt; however, financial stability is also a prerequisite for any recovery in growth.
But whoever speaks of solidarity, of financial stability, and budgetary discipline—since these naturally go hand in hand—must also speak of solidarity.
We believe it is important to ensure this dual movement toward financial stability, budgetary discipline, and solidarity.
One final word about democracy.
Beware of undermining our democratic institutions.

Rather than considering recourse to a judicial mechanism to impose sanctions in cases of budgetary deviations, we should instead focus on mechanisms of oversight and sanctions exercised by democratic institutions.

To be perfectly frank, I am not the spokesperson for the French Minister of Foreign Affairs, and quite frankly, even though I belong to the same political party as him, I am not constantly, I would say, in a position to relay the statements of the French Minister of Foreign Affairs.
To be once again completely frank, I am not aware of any recent statements by Alain Juppé to that effect.
I simply believe that France's current position is to continually reaffirm that the euro is truly the heart of our European construction, that the preservation of the euro,

I mean the preservation of this shared project and the preservation of the European Union. In this effort to safeguard the euro, we must of course look to the future, consider the protection of younger generations, and ensure the preservation of peace on our continent.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, tomorrow we will vote for the third time on the new instrument for development cooperation.
the development initiative, which provides accompanying measures in the banana sector for ACP exporting countries following the reduction of their tariff preferences, the ACP Banana Agreement.
This assistance program has a budget of 190 million euros and targets ten ACP countries until 2013.
It is of crucial importance for our African and Caribbean partners, given that the banana sector holds considerable economic and social significance in these countries.
Although none of the Parliament's claims regarding the application of delegated acts have been incorporated into this instrument, I regret this strongly.
We have nonetheless managed to achieve progress.
Indeed, the text sets clear objectives and precise criteria for allocating resources to the Commission.
The Commission also issued a statement specifying the methodology used.

Moreover, we have a statement from the Council on the next multiannual financial framework, specifying that it will duly take into account delegated acts in external assistance instruments after 2013.
Finally, in its resolution, Parliament clarifies—and this point strikes me as crucial—that this in no way sets a precedent.

And I would like to tell you that I have just returned from Togo, where the 22nd session of the ACP-EU Parliamentary Assembly took place, and I believe I can confidently say that our ACP partners are counting on us given the urgency of the situation.
The funds should have been released two years ago.
We must be aware of the regrettable consequences for our ACP partners if the ICD BAM were rejected.
The situation of certain farmers is dramatic; we can no longer wait and must act accordingly.
I therefore count on you to support the text. Thank you.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, our coordinator, Tissbergmann, unfortunately cannot be here this evening for personal reasons.
However, I know he fully shares my analysis on this matter.

The negotiations on this legislative package were long and tedious, and although the outcome regarding the human rights instruments and ICI Plus is satisfactory, we are still far from achieving our goals concerning the banana instrument.

Thus, the compromise reached on this text dismisses in a single stroke the mandate given to our negotiation team, which was to ensure that Parliament would be placed on equal footing with the Council on all programming decisions concerning the selection of strategic priorities and financial allocations.
Because this concerns indeed the powers of our Assembly and the very spirit of the Treaty of Lisbon.
The choice of programming priorities is a highly political decision on which our Assembly must have its say.
By categorically refusing to recognize any form of parliamentary oversight and resorting to shameful blackmail, the Council has once again demonstrated a condemnable ideological retreat.
But Member States must understand that the European Union's external action is now a shared competence among our institutions.
That is why our Parliament must remain sovereign and reject the proposal on the banana instrument.
This is the only way we have of showing the Council and the Commission that the Parliament wishes for greater transparency in community decisions.
If we lose this battle, absurdly, we would have to wait for a new treaty to finally obtain the powers already provided for by the Lisbon Treaty.
Mr. President, at the risk of offending those of you who are most federalist, I believe that a defense policy is eminently a national policy.
It meets the specific strategic, diplomatic, and security needs of each nation.
Sharing capabilities is nothing other than a loss of sovereignty. The single market for military equipment is a vast joke when certain member states believe that
Defense is guaranteed by Washington, with American equipment being prioritized, Europe being unable, in this field as in so many others, to enforce community preference.

With regard to the shared security challenges facing the member states of the Union, nothing exists that cannot be resolved through intergovernmental cooperation, without any involvement from Brussels.

I regret, but entrusting these areas to Ms. Ashton would amount to acknowledging that Moody's and Standard & Poor's have legitimacy in judging our economic policies, and accepting that the Mercosur bloc could impose a purely German Europe on twenty-seven peoples—an utter mental aberration.

For thirty years, and even more so since the disappearance of the Soviet threat, defense budgets have been the first to be sacrificed on the altar of fiscal austerity, to the point of rendering most of our military tools ineffective.

deficient or outdated, and this for purely financial reasons, each party waiting for the neighbor or the supposed American elder brother to make the efforts that it is no longer willing to make itself.
But you're mistaken about the voice.

Only by returning to independent and resolute national policies will we individually be able to acquire the necessary means not only for our own security and national interests, but also to cooperate in pooling these resources to address common challenges.
An accumulation of weaknesses and shortcomings has never made a strength.

Mr. President, I wished to express the support of the French delegation of the PPE group for the European Treaty on Economic Stability, Coordination and Governance, and its backing of the main conclusions reached by the Council.
Let us not be deceived by this.
This treaty represents an important step towards greater economic and budgetary integration, towards greater financial stability.
Let us say it again here.
Sustainable revival of growth and employment is impossible without the restoration of our public finances.
Of course, growth cannot be decreed; it is not a treaty alone that can revive growth.
But a treaty, that is to say, a set of rules and mutual commitments, can provide trust and accountability.

As the president of the Socialist group has deemed it appropriate to introduce political opportunism into this chamber, and by referring to the upcoming French presidential campaign and mentioning the Socialist candidate in that French presidential election, I wished to respond.
We, the PPE, consider utterly irresponsible the statements made by the French Socialist candidate who declared his intention to renegotiate the treaty concluded by
renegotiate what and with whom.
These words are completely irresponsible.
They pay little attention to the word of the French State, to the respect of the commitments made by the French State.

Madame Ashton, Madagascar, one of the poorest countries in the world, is experiencing a political crisis whose resolution will depend on the implementation of the roadmap leading up to the presidential and legislative elections.
The crossroads of this country is negative.
Under normal circumstances, our contribution amounts to about 50 percent of this country's budget.
The European Union has rightly suspended its budgetary support for the ninth and tenth FED plans, but not for the MDGs.
Meanwhile, the population suffers, and businesses established in Madagascar are sometimes compelled to contribute, despite themselves.

While supporting your position, we urge you to exert your full influence with the SADC to resolve as soon as possible the legal and judicial complications surrounding the return of the former president, which constitutes one of the most sensitive aspects of implementing this roadmap.

I would appreciate your views on this matter, and I would also like to inform you that the newly established European Union-Madagascar Friendship Group at the European Parliament is available to work with you and the Commission in the interest of the Malagasy people and in defense of the values of our Union.

Mr. President, dear colleagues, I would like to commend the rapporteur, Mr. Iturguyès, for the quality of his work, for the extensive negotiation effort he has had to undertake, and for the patience he has demonstrated.

We have finally reached the vote on this report, which is extremely important, as it enables us to bring to this chamber, before the Commission and the Council, the difficulties and concerns of our fellow citizens.
Here is further evidence, if any were needed, of the effectiveness of the right of petition in Europe.
Petitions concerning the environment are predominant, and waste represents a significant concern for our fellow citizens.
This topic must address a social issue.
We consume in Tuva.
We are only now beginning to realize that what we do not consume, we throw away.
This awareness must be firmly reinforced among citizens, that is clear.
But this report also serves as a reminder to the member states.
It is nonetheless concerning to see that states appear to be losing control of the situation.
Only six member states have transposed the waste directive, the waste framework directive, on time.
The number of complaints received on this matter sometimes reveals a lack of transparency in the selection of landfill locations.
Finally, and this is the most alarming, organized crime appears to be doing as it pleases.
A market worth 75 billion euros inevitably attracts interest.
States must send a strong signal.
Sanctions against companies that disclose public contracts for personal gain must be increased.
This report serves as a warning signal to states, but the European Commission must also fulfill its role by ensuring the proper implementation of waste directives.
This file must be made visible, but also credible.
If no one plays their part, we will continue to waste time and money on verification and re-verification procedures that may last for years.
Let us heed the petitioners' message and put an end to this waste more quickly.
Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Rapporteur, I would first like to congratulate Mr. Nicholson for the courage he has shown in facing the Council and the Commission.
Nevertheless, I fear that what we have arrived at does not allow for a balance between producers and industrialists.
This agreement reminds me of that illusion we all know, the one children tell us about, of a paradise where vegetarian lions would graze on grass beside lambs.
I don't believe it for a second. Since 2003, Mrs. Fischer Boel has done everything possible to ideologically dismantle the dairy quotas.
In October 2009, the Court of Auditors nonetheless acknowledged the effectiveness of these milk quotas.

Today, we are in a situation that will be even more dramatic, and we can see what is currently happening in Denmark, where the most solid farms, despite increased production, are all facing bankruptcy, with land prices having dropped by 40%.
Do we want this for the European level?
I say no; production must be managed with the involvement of public authorities. Thank you.
Madam Commissioner, the data protection standards of third countries still differ significantly from European standards.
It is nevertheless our duty to ensure that our minimum European standards of protection, which we uphold, are respected and guaranteed to European citizens.
It is essential that personal data be processed fairly and collected for specified and legitimate purposes.

European citizens must be guaranteed the right of access and correction regarding personal data concerning them, as well as the right to bring a legal remedy in the event of a violation of their rights.
Action must be taken to allow European and international standards to coexist and to resolve a potentially harmful legal conflict for European citizens.
European legislation alone is not sufficient.
It must be structured and coexist intelligently with international standards, as we cannot disregard our duties and commitments to European citizens.
This situation echoes other debates taking place within this Assembly.
It reminds us of the relevance of initiatives such as the establishment of a European programme for monitoring terrorist financing.

Such a system would enable us to have at our disposal a tool that has proven its effectiveness in the United States, while fully respecting domestic law and thus ensuring the protection of personal data of European citizens.
European institutions must continue their commitment in this regard and fully uphold their standards of protection. Thank you.
Mr. President, first of all, thank you to the rapporteurs for their work.

In response to the crisis, the European Union has spent over a year strengthening its economic governance to prevent macroeconomic imbalances and excessive budgetary deficits threatening the European economy, yet this has not prevented either social exclusion or the rise in unemployment.
It is time—indeed, it is now urgent—that the objectives of the Europe 2020 strategy become an absolute priority for the European Commission and for the Member States, in a perspective of growth.

Each state will be required to implement a national employment plan, meaning that emphasis should be placed on issues such as integration, training, vocational reorientation, microcredit, apprenticeships, paid internships, and so on.

Moreover, and as Commissioner Andor reminded us just now, we have 23 million unemployed people in Europe, as well as 23 million small and medium-sized enterprises and 23 billion euros in European funds dedicated to employment that remain unused.
We could almost make it a strategy 23.
Not to mention the 60 billion euros in unallocated cohesion funds.
In Denmark and the Netherlands, social initiatives have been implemented and proven effective, based on flexicurity.
Let us draw inspiration from it.

It also means that our social policy as a whole must be better tailored, with more favorable social incentives than those linked to unemployment, and active support for all socially responsible enterprises.
We had poverty reduction targets to achieve by 2020, yet we already know they will not be met due to the lack of strong and appropriate measures taken by the Member States.

Our role as parliamentarians must therefore be fully exercised, and it is our responsibility to demand that the same energy devoted to implementing strong economic governance also be directed toward ambitious, effective, and binding social governance measures.
Madam President, undoubtedly, the cause of women owes much to Europe.
The principle of gender equality was already enshrined in the Treaty of Rome and has been consistently reaffirmed ever since in numerous treaties.
Gender equality is truly a fundamental value of our European project.
As a result, Europe has always pursued gender equality and the advancement of women with great determination.
Little by little, progress was made.
Numerous legislative instruments have been adopted, and guidelines have been established.
Here, within our Parliament itself, for many years now, there has been an extremely active and vigilant Committee on Women's Rights and Gender Equality.
An European Institute for Gender Equality has been established.
Programs are being implemented, and financial resources are being approved year after year.
More recently, on March 2, we had the opportunity to celebrate, for its second edition, the European Day for Equal Treatment, Equal Pay
an European Equal Pay Day. Undoubtedly, therefore, the legislative framework exists, and European commitment has been concretely demonstrated on several occasions.
Yet it must be acknowledged that the struggle remains ever relevant—the struggle for gender equality, and in particular for equal pay.
On average, across all countries of the European Union, there is always a wage gap of the order of
At equal work, women always earn 17% less than men.
This is of course entirely unacceptable.
The Women's Committee of the Parliament is currently working on a draft resolution calling on the European Commission to revise the 2006 directive on women's equality.
The issue, which is one of the most pressing challenges and at the very heart of European women's concerns and demands in all our societies.
On another matter, that of establishing quotas—quotas both within the decision-making bodies of major corporations and within political life—I come from a country, France, which long ago adopted
ten years, binding rules on quotas in political life, and more recently, about a year ago, a law establishing quotas in the boards of directors of major corporations.
France is not the only country, of course, to have adopted such legislation.
What is the assessment after several years of application in one case, a few months in the other, forced to acknowledge that things have shifted, that progress has been made?
Thus, a necessary evil.
Quotas do make a difference.

Madam Commissioner, you have recently taken an important step toward establishing quotas at the European level in the decision-making bodies of major companies by launching this extensive public consultation aimed at gathering opinions on the actions to be undertaken, and potentially on legislative measures to be adopted.
On this matter, you will have my full and complete support.
Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, on the occasion of this resolution, allow me to address two issues: first, the challenge of water management worldwide and in Europe.
an Europe, and then a question to the European Commission so that measures can be adopted to address a number of our concerns relating to the common agricultural policy.
The issue, as we know, is that water is the major concern of the twenty-first century.
It is because we will have answers that we will have peace in the world and in Europe.
We are aware of the stakes, we are aware of the stakes in terms of management, we are aware of the stakes in terms of food security.
And, on this occasion, as our colleague Rivasi has reminded us, too many purely commercial concerns lie at the heart of the discussion that has unfolded in Marseille in recent days.
A question: why is there no coherence between water management and water-related resources and the Common Agricultural Policy, since
Nothing in the reform of the CAP is specified regarding this management, as our colleague Sarboud just mentioned.
Give us answers.
Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would first like to thank the rapporteur and our assistants for the work accomplished.
This report now fully lives up to its name, namely, removing barriers to citizenship.
Europe is a magnificent project, but it is all too frequently observed that it has not yet been fully defined.
Take, for example, the economic crisis.
It revealed our weaknesses and enabled us to strengthen more solidly the institutions regulating our single market.
For the European citizen, it is the same.
The Petitions Committee, a true office for grievances, records the complaints of our fellow citizens every day.
In three days, the European Citizens' Initiative will complete this framework.
Despite all our policies calling for mobility, engagement, European values, and exchanges, there remain far too many obstacles to the exercise of citizenship.
A citizen who does not feel European is a citizen who will not vote, a citizen who will not perceive the value of Europe in their everyday life.
Along with my colleagues from the PPE, we have made numerous proposals—more than any other group—and I would like to thank them for this.
It is a sense of European belonging and identity that we must recreate and sustain through these measures.
Our Europe must be a Europe that protects.
Commissioner Viviane Reding has put forward twenty-five very concrete proposals in favor of citizens.
They are essential for restoring trust between the citizen and Europe.
However, the proposals contained in this report reflect other expectations of our fellow citizens: online shopping, relocations, children's rights, employment, health, and safety.
citizen participation, healthcare abroad, twinning initiatives, minority rights, etc. We still find it too difficult to fully benefit from the capabilities offered by Europe.
Rights are exercised, claims are heard.
We are the ones who carry this forward here.
The European Year of Citizenship in 2013 must demonstrate to citizens that, with free movement, there is also free exercise of rights across Europe.
Mr. President, I believe that taxation is an interesting tool for shaping behavior.

I believe energy consumption should align with an indicative price signal for the consumer, and above all, I think that to avoid any distortion in the internal market, we must strive to bring our tax practices closer together.
The principle of proportionality among the various levels of taxation on energy products therefore appears to me to be a necessity.
In the case under consideration, for example, this would make it possible to put an end to the current diesel tourism, which is not an acceptable situation.
I therefore share the philosophy underlying the Commission's proposal for revising the directive.

Nevertheless, if we are to achieve our objectives regarding CO2 emission reductions or energy efficiency, our collective decisions must on the other hand not penalize citizens or sectors particularly exposed to pollution.

The volatility of prices on global markets, and I am thinking, for example, of the agricultural sector. The proposed directive will undoubtedly have an impact—albeit differing according to the tax levels in each Member State—on diesel prices.
The question is, who, in the final analysis, will have to bear this price increase?
Are there truly practical, realistic, and consumer-accessible alternatives?
As the fictitious rapporteur in the environmental committee, I support an ambitious yet above all sustainable stance for everyone.

I therefore advocate for greater progressivity and for the implementation of the proposal and its principle of proportionality to take effect in 2030, provided that a new impact assessment is conducted, updating the one upon which the Commission's proposal is based, which was nevertheless carried out five years ago.

A month earlier, right here, we held a debate on the wave of layoffs across Europe triggered by the economic crisis, particularly at Arcelor, a multinational company operating in nine European member states, which is restructuring its production facilities in Europe in response to declining steel demand.

Following the shutdown of two blast furnaces in Belgium and Luxembourg, and the temporary suspension of one blast furnace in France, it is estimated that nearly six direct jobs will be at risk in 2012.
Beyond unacceptable practices, such as stop-and-go, which ought to be better regulated, the broader issue of European steelmaking and adaptation to change demands extremely urgent responses.

Six months ago, the Commission informed us of the need to carry out, in light of the economic crisis, a new consultation exercise for responsible management of restructuring, in cooperation with social partners and public authorities.
My question is simple: six months later, where are we?

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues, I would first like to firmly dismiss the unfounded allegations regarding the circulation in France of between 500,000 and one million counterfeit biometric passports allegedly circulating in our country.
France takes the risks of fraud related to identity documents very seriously.
She does not take offenses in this matter lightly.
At the initiative of its government, identity theft is a criminal offense punishable by one year in prison and a fine of 15 euros.
The conditions for issuing identity documents have been simplified, enabling prefectures to allocate additional resources to combat this fraud.
Thus, in each prefecture, a fraud liaison officer is designated.
Furthermore, the provisions ensuring the secure issuance of identity documents have been strengthened.
For example, civil status data provided may be verified across administrative services, as far as possible through digital means, in order to minimize the risk of falsification.
Certain birth certificates are also issued on secure paper.
The same rigor applies to the justification-of-address trap.
The Ministry of the Interior has committed to enhancing the security of proof of address documents by incorporating a barcode.

The standard processing of a title application includes authorized access to relevant files, comparison of all documents submitted in previous title applications, and, where necessary, authentication of supporting documents.

The security of title issuance is further ensured by the applicant's two personal appearances: once at the time of application submission, and again at the time of title collection, along with the capture of two digital fingerprints.
And without even mentioning the enhanced checks required for renewing lost or stolen documents, in comparison with data retained by prefectures—I conclude.

Moreover, France alerts international cooperation bodies, and in particular Interpol, that the passports issued, lost, or stolen should not be used for fraudulent purposes. I conclude, Mr. President; I am almost finished.
Dear colleagues, France, like the rest of the European Union, is committed.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, first of all, a observation: our fellow citizens increasingly wish to find and consume foods produced locally and in season, for reasons of health and quality, among others.
We must find ways to enable farmers to respond effectively and promptly to this growing demand.

We must support the establishment of local infrastructure for producers, such as maintaining local slaughterhouses, which, while ensuring rigorous sanitary oversight, enable the development of local short supply chains and strengthen direct sales.
Support for local agriculture and direct sales must help producers market their products and make them known to consumers.
Strengthening these direct relationships will enable farmers to retain a larger share of the added value generated on their farms.

Direct sales within the farm operation should be encouraged and facilitated, and collective initiatives by farmers who wish to establish shared sales points should be supported, enabling local consumers to access a broader range of products.

The European Commission should facilitate the emergence, sustainability, and development of direct delivery systems to consumers, such as Amap in France, which enable around three farmers to supply sixty households.
Finally, Member States must be able to add additional criteria, in particular regarding farm size, production volume, or the traditional nature of the product, in order to define local agriculture.

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, I welcome the fact that this amended budget concerning the funding of ITER, which has been adopted, received a broad majority in the Budgets Committee on 29 March last.
By funding this additional cost, we avoid discarding a strategic fundamental research project in which the European Union plays a leading role, and we uphold our international commitments.
We recall that none of the seven Euratom community partners has questioned its overall contribution to the project.
Japan, despite the Fukushima tragedy, has decided to strictly honor its budgetary commitments to ITER.
Without this agreement, hundreds of researchers would have had to suspend their work, and the ITER project would have suffered detrimental delays.
Today, a thousand people work for ITER in Kadaraš.
From 2015 to 2017, the construction of the buildings and assembly of the machine could involve up to four people at this site.
To conclude, Mr. President, ITER is a source of pride for Europe and a symbol of our excellence.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. rapporteur, thank you for the work we have accomplished together.
The European Union needs a manifesto, a shared political vision on the major challenges related to the environment, by 2020 and beyond.
This is precisely the value of launching a seventh environmental action programme by synergizing existing financial frameworks and mobilizing new sources of funding.
This program must ensure coherence and coordination between the European Union's environmental strategies and sectoral policies, whether agriculture
Fishing, research and innovation, health, transport, regional development, territorial planning, and urban policies, of course.
Despite the economic crisis afflicting the European Union, we must not succumb to a short-term outlook and abandon all ambition in the environmental and climate fields.
Inaction would be fatal.
The stakes are too high, particularly with regard to the efficient use of our resources and the valuation of natural capital.
We must therefore firmly invest in the sustainable economy and eco-innovation, sources of growth, competitiveness, and employment.
It is our responsibility to promote eco-design, recycling, and environmental product labeling in order to prevent waste and misguided consumption choices.
We cannot overlook this necessary environmental pedagogy for European citizens.
The environmental action program is a tool for political planning, but it is also a communication instrument.
It must provide the necessary clarity and predictability for national and local authorities, citizens, as well as entrepreneurs and investors, regarding the European Union's environmental choices.
European regulation must not become a punishment or a straitjacket.
Mr. President, dear colleagues, in the face of the profound changes to which our world is currently subject, the Union must reform its Generalized System of Preferences.
Like any compromise, this one is no exception to the rule.
While satisfactory from a strictly commercial standpoint, it is far from perfect. We must keep in mind that this regulation is intended for developing countries.
By developing its new paradigm of differentiation, the Commission states that it aims to refocus its action in favor of the poorest countries.
This does not appear entirely tenable at first glance.
To this end, the Commission has nonetheless developed a strictly accounting vision based solely on the criterion of GDP per capita.
One can clearly see the limitation of this approach.
Despite many middle-income developing countries whose economies are poorly diversified and vulnerable to climate shocks.
The compromise reached with the Council does not address this issue, which is why I urge my colleagues to vote in favor of the three amendments to be submitted on this text.
Mr. Commissioner, the issue of the availability of public consultations in the various languages of the European Union is not new, unfortunately.

This is a crucial question, in my opinion, which is why I have not only submitted several written questions to the European Commission since 2008, but have also personally addressed written inquiries to Mr. Barroso.

Indeed, the Commission must comply with the principle of equal treatment and access to information for all European citizens, as reminded by the Court of First Instance of the European Communities in its judgment of 20 November 2008.
Mr. President, I was entirely unsatisfied with the European Commission's response stating that public consultations do not aim to provide a fully representative picture of all opinions.
