According to this response, the Commission takes into account the fact that certain positions and viewpoints may be overrepresented at the expense of others.
That is not a response.
I am considering the criteria that would allow measuring the overrepresentation of the European population.
If written consultations are not overrepresented due solely to the availability in English, for example, they fail to achieve their purpose of consulting European citizens.
Thus, the result provides a misleading indication and should therefore not be used by the European Commission.
If we wish to preserve written consultations as a foundation for the European Commission's legislative proposals, we must ensure a democratic, transparent, and fair system for all citizens.
Thus, if the European Commission conducts written consultations, it must assume its responsibilities and provide the necessary resources for multilingualism and the translation of questionnaires.
To conclude, I would like to add that multilingualism is not the only obstacle.

Mr. President, dear colleagues, I am speaking on behalf of Maurice Ponga, the rapporteur for opinion of the Development Committee on Mr. Gallagher's report.
It seemed important to emphasize that when a nation seeks to sanction a country engaged in unsustainable fishing practices, it must take into account that country's level of development.

Indeed, in the name of the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, it is, in my view, essential that measures adopted in the field of fisheries do not undermine the development plans and strategies established by the European Union in these countries.
Mr. Gallagher's draft report takes up this important principle for our committee by stipulating that measures must not be unjustified, nor even discriminatory.

I therefore urge the European Commission, when adopting such measures, to respect the principle of policy coherence with development objectives, and to duly take into account the level of development and vulnerability of countries.
The third reform of the Common Fisheries Policy must not once again be carried out at the expense of fishermen's interests.

No, not all fishermen are these plunderers of the sea, solely responsible for the poor health of fish stocks, as environmental organizations and the European Commission claim when they encompass all categories of fishing.

It goes without saying that fish stocks must be preserved, but we must also allow our fishermen to earn a living from their activity and safeguard the economic fabric, vitality, and cultural identity of our coastal regions.
Indeed, the European executive's main measures will, once again, aim at reducing the number of fishermen in order to achieve the mythical maximum sustainable yield.
I recall that the French fleet has decreased by more than 50% since 1980.

The Commission notably proposes the introduction of individual transferable quotas, seen as a guarantee of industrialization and sectoral consolidation, or alternatively, a complete ban on discharges, which professionals consider unrealistic.
This reform proposal may suit the industrial fleet, but as currently formulated, it would mean the programmed demise of what remains of local and artisanal fishing.

We all know here that the European Union provides more than half of the world's total public development assistance, and yet, as the 2015 deadline for the MDGs approaches, even if some goals appear to be
Affected, we must ask ourselves how to improve our assistance, making it more consistent with our trade policy, our agricultural policy, and our foreign policy.
The future of European development policy must be based on clear, measurable commitments, with a short- and medium-term timeline.

The economic crisis, climate change, the rising influence of emerging countries, and land grabbing compel us to establish new priorities to ensure food security, access to energy resources, and access to healthcare.

Certainly, a European consensus exists, but we must rethink European and international environmental and social institutional systems by drawing inspiration from bodies that function effectively in decision-making, such as the WTO, for example.
Finally, and above all, we must explain to our fellow citizens that the development of third countries is not merely charity and solidarity, but also a source of development for us.
One need only look at the markets opening up for Europeans in emerging countries such as China, Brazil, and India.
2015 will also provide an opportunity to better account for the geostrategic developments of recent years, as the emerging countries of today were, not long ago, developing nations.
Now that these countries have become indispensable economic powers, they must also contribute to international aid.
The European Union must be the driving force behind responsible development aid at both the European and international levels.

The year 2015 will be more than a deadline; it will be a unique opportunity to rethink development aid, not merely as financial assistance, but as support for responsible development initiatives, both socially and environmentally.

I therefore join my colleagues from the other groups, as well as those from the Economic and Social Committee and the Committee of the Regions, in calling for 2015 to be designated the European Year of Development, and above all, of responsible development.
Mr. President, in my view, the Commission's agenda for change raises numerous concerns, the main one being the practical implementation of the principle of differentiation.

The Commission wishes that, starting from 2014, the selection of countries in which the European Union will channel its development aid be based on the mechanical application of the single criterion of GDP per capita.
As Ms. Ashton has reminded us, these issues are not merely accounting matters, but warrant a genuine political debate regarding the definition of our priorities in international cooperation.
Moreover, in my view, this differentiated approach is not consistent with the spirit of the Cotonou Agreement.

Mr. President, this year the Petitions Committee has acquired the right to be automatically associated with the hearings on European citizens' initiatives.
It is, for me, the clear signal of the year 2011 for this Commission.
Citizens already had a Commission to which they could turn in the event of a violation of their rights, but they can now also do so when they have proposals for improving European legislation.
I hope that the Committee on Petitions will thus increasingly be regarded as the preferred point of contact for citizens within the European Parliament.
This issue of consideration is also found in the findings of the Committee on Petitions' positions.
Too often, we are approached by citizens facing legal entanglements on which we wish to take a stance, yet while the issues are European,
Although solutions should be European, we remain powerless because subsidiarity still does not align with European solutions due to a lack of national will.

Despite the obvious difficulties faced by many parents whose children are born from binational unions, we remain in Parliament still powerless to act.

My proposal that all European citizens should have the right to a national ombudsman, particularly one from Germany, which submits a large number of petitions in the style of France's Defender of Rights, has had no effect.
Yet, in the case of cross-border divorces, this remains a conciliatory solution, a problematic European issue, and a national resolution.
In many cases, European law appears powerless to meet citizens' expectations.
